nodes.label: 2. Research Method
nodes.text: 2. Research Method

nodes.label: This research project adopts the Design Science Research (DSR) method, as BA mod
nodes.text: This research project adopts the Design Science Research (DSR) method, as BA modelling is naturally complex and organised in layers.

nodes.label: DSR works well for this kind of problem, as it helps break down complex issues i
nodes.text: DSR works well for this kind of problem, as it helps break down complex issues into smaller, more manageable parts clearly and logically.

nodes.label: This project follows the structured DSR hierarchy proposed by Tuunanen et al. [2
nodes.text: This project follows the structured DSR hierarchy proposed by Tuunanen et al. [20] as illustrated in Figure 1.

nodes.label: The DSR Method adopted from Tuunanen et al. [20] The adopted DSR has four stages
nodes.text: The DSR Method adopted from Tuunanen et al. [20] The adopted DSR has four stages (Figure 1).

nodes.label: Firstly, we identified the research problem, which is the static and manual natu
nodes.text: Firstly, we identified the research problem, which is the static and manual nature of traditional BA methods, producing static BA artefacts that become outdated quickly and are unable to keep pace with evolving business decision-making.

nodes.label: Secondly, our goal was to explore how agentic AI can be leveraged to automate BA
nodes.text: Secondly, our goal was to explore how agentic AI can be leveraged to automate BA artefacts.

nodes.label: We applied creative thinking to produce a tentative initial design of ABMA.
nodes.text: We applied creative thinking to produce a tentative initial design of ABMA.

nodes.label: Comprehensive research was conducted to develop the ABMA design.
nodes.text: Comprehensive research was conducted to develop the ABMA design.

nodes.label: Thirdly, the design and development are performed iteratively to evolve ABMA.
nodes.text: Thirdly, the design and development are performed iteratively to evolve ABMA.

nodes.label: Finally, the results of the scenario illustration will be presented and discusse
nodes.text: Finally, the results of the scenario illustration will be presented and discussed to conclude the project.

nodes.label: 3. Agentic AI-enabled Business Modelling Architecture (ABMA)
nodes.text: 3. Agentic AI-enabled Business Modelling Architecture (ABMA)

nodes.label: 3.1. Overview
nodes.text: 3.1. Overview

nodes.label: The ABMA (see Figure 2) presents a modular and AI-driven system designed to auto
nodes.text: The ABMA (see Figure 2) presents a modular and AI-driven system designed to automate the generation of BA artefacts through the orchestration of autonomous agents.

nodes.label: This system architecture is built upon the principles of agentic AI, natural lan
nodes.text: This system architecture is built upon the principles of agentic AI, natural language interaction, and enterprise knowledge contextualisation to overcome the static and labor-intensive nature of traditional BA methods.

nodes.label: The architecture comprises five core layers: user interaction, context extractio
nodes.text: The architecture comprises five core layers: user interaction, context extraction, orchestration, processing, and knowledge layer.

nodes.label: The underlying cognitive framework of ABMA mirrors the way humans’ reason throug
nodes.text: The underlying cognitive framework of ABMA mirrors the way humans’ reason through complex tasks.

nodes.label: How each layer interacts and operates is detailed through a demonstration in Sec
nodes.text: How each layer interacts and operates is detailed through a demonstration in Section 5.

nodes.label: Figure 2. Agentic-AI-enabled BA modelling architecture (Content generation layer
nodes.text: Figure 2. Agentic-AI-enabled BA modelling architecture (Content generation layer)

nodes.label: 3.2. ABMA Component Interaction
nodes.text: 3.2. ABMA Component Interaction

nodes.label: When architecture is implemented in the real world, the BA artefact generation p
nodes.text: When architecture is implemented in the real world, the BA artefact generation process is initiated by the user upon submitting a natural language query through the prompt interface within the user interface.

nodes.label: There is also a feedback interface for input refinement based on system output.
nodes.text: There is also a feedback interface for input refinement based on system output.

nodes.label: For example, a query can request the generation of capability maps for a specifi
nodes.text: For example, a query can request the generation of capability maps for a specific industry, region, or business function, as detailed in Section 5.

nodes.label: Secondly, the natural language query is passed on to the entity agent, which ser
nodes.text: Secondly, the natural language query is passed on to the entity agent, which serves as the first point of analysis.

nodes.label: It parses the query to extract contextual information such as industry type, art
nodes.text: It parses the query to extract contextual information such as industry type, artefact category (e.g. operational capability, strategic capability), and geographic or regulatory scope.

nodes.label: The entity agent may delegate tasks to LLM to handle ambiguous or unstructured i
nodes.text: The entity agent may delegate tasks to LLM to handle ambiguous or unstructured input.

nodes.label: The entity agent is also linked to a reference ontology, providing semantic grou
nodes.text: The entity agent is also linked to a reference ontology, providing semantic grounding for the identified entities.

nodes.label: This enables the agent to resolve any disambiguation and ensure that the entitie
nodes.text: This enables the agent to resolve any disambiguation and ensure that the entities identified are aligned with the operational domain.

nodes.label: A specific industry or organisation can have its ontology defined and linked for
nodes.text: A specific industry or organisation can have its ontology defined and linked for this purpose.

nodes.label: Thirdly, the parsed entities are handed over to the orchestration agent, which i
nodes.text: Thirdly, the parsed entities are handed over to the orchestration agent, which is responsible for routing the query to a particular agent based on the identified entities.

nodes.label: The agent responsible for the task will generate the artefact in response to the
nodes.text: The agent responsible for the task will generate the artefact in response to the user's query, interacting with the LLM.

nodes.label: To ensure semantic accuracy and organisational alignment, the agent will validat
nodes.text: To ensure semantic accuracy and organisational alignment, the agent will validate the artefacts generated by the LLM against both the domain ontology and the enterprise knowledge graph (EKG).

nodes.label: The input knowledge for generating capability lists is derived from user queries
nodes.text: The input knowledge for generating capability lists is derived from user queries, enriched by reference ontologies, and validated against EKG to ensure semantic accuracy, contextual alignment, and organisational relevance.

nodes.label: This hybrid approach combines the generative strength of the LLM with the semant
nodes.text: This hybrid approach combines the generative strength of the LLM with the semantic control of structured enterprise knowledge, resulting in accurate and context-aware BA modelling, which leads to increased trust in the generated model.

nodes.label: These interactions are illustrated in Figure 2.
nodes.text: These interactions are illustrated in Figure 2.

nodes.label: Finally, once the agent completes its reasoning, the structured output is return
nodes.text: Finally, once the agent completes its reasoning, the structured output is returned to the user for feedback, refinement, or re-query.

nodes.label: 4. Scenario Illustration: Agentic AI-enabled Business Capability
nodes.text: 4. Scenario Illustration: Agentic AI-enabled Business Capability

nodes.label: Modelling Process: To demonstrate and validate the practical feasibility of the
nodes.text: Modelling Process: To demonstrate and validate the practical feasibility of the proposed ABMA, we have developed a proof-of-concept implementation of an agentic AI-based business capability modelling process using AutoGen 0.2, an open-source multi-agent orchestration framework developed by Microsoft.

nodes.label: AutoGen 0.2 facilitates the coordination of autonomous agents through structured
nodes.text: AutoGen 0.2 facilitates the coordination of autonomous agents through structured dialogues and dynamic task delegation, making it well-suited for realising the modularity and autonomy envisioned in the ABMA.

nodes.label: For demonstration purposes, we have implemented this through the OpenAI API GPT-
nodes.text: For demonstration purposes, we have implemented this through the OpenAI API GPT- 4o.

nodes.label: Figure 3 illustrates our design in AutoGen 0.2.
nodes.text: Figure 3 illustrates our design in AutoGen 0.2.

nodes.label: AutoGen 0.2 implementation To understand the value of ontology and EKG in the pr
nodes.text: AutoGen 0.2 implementation To understand the value of ontology and EKG in the proposed architecture, we implement this scenario over two configurations. • Configuration 1: entity agent and capability agent interact with LLM only • Configuration 2: entity agent and capability agent access ontology and EKG In Configuration 2, the Capsicum BA ontology and EKG, provided by the industry partner, are used in our demonstration [21].

nodes.label: 4.1. Configuration 1: ToT-Prompted Agent Reasoning
nodes.text: 4.1. Configuration 1: ToT-Prompted Agent Reasoning

nodes.label: In the initial phase of experimentation (as shown in Figure 4), we created an en
nodes.text: In the initial phase of experimentation (as shown in Figure 4), we created an entity agent and a capability agent.

nodes.label: In this configuration, we employed the reasoning-based ToT prompting technique.
nodes.text: In this configuration, we employed the reasoning-based ToT prompting technique.

nodes.label: ToT enhances the reasoning capabilities of language models by allowing them to e
nodes.text: ToT enhances the reasoning capabilities of language models by allowing them to explore multiple solution paths in parallel and evaluate intermediate thoughts before arriving at a final response.

nodes.label: The entity agent was provided with simple instructions to parse user queries int
nodes.text: The entity agent was provided with simple instructions to parse user queries into structured entities: BA artefact, industry, region and levels.

nodes.label: The capability agent received structured entities as input and was tasked with s
nodes.text: The capability agent received structured entities as input and was tasked with selecting capability definitions and composing a capability map.

nodes.label: At this stage, semantic grounding was limited to rule-based inference and a manu
nodes.text: At this stage, semantic grounding was limited to rule-based inference and a manually curated ToT prompt technique, without leveraging external knowledge graphs.

nodes.label: Figure 4. Config 1 prompt-based BCM The extracted portion (claim management capa
nodes.text: Figure 4. Config 1 prompt-based BCM The extracted portion (claim management capability) of the response from the complete capability map is shown in Table 1 Query: Generate a 5-level capability map for the insurance industry in Japan.

nodes.label: Table 1. Response to the prompt-based BCM
nodes.text: Table 1. Response to the prompt-based BCM

nodes.label: 4.2. Configuration 2: EKG Integration via Stardog
nodes.text: 4.2. Configuration 2: EKG Integration via Stardog

nodes.label: In the second configuration (see Figure 5), semantic validity was introduced thr
nodes.text: In the second configuration (see Figure 5), semantic validity was introduced through the integration of an EKG, developed by our industry partner, Capsifi.

nodes.label: This graph is hosted on Stardog, a graph database platform that supports RDF/SPA
nodes.text: This graph is hosted on Stardog, a graph database platform that supports RDF/SPARQL-based queries and semantic reasoning.

nodes.label: The entity agent was extended to query the Stardog-based EKG, enabling more accu
nodes.text: The entity agent was extended to query the Stardog-based EKG, enabling more accurate parsing of user queries by grounding extracted entities in organisational semantics (e.g., industry taxonomies, regional classifications, artefacts).

nodes.label: The capability agent used the same EKG to validate whether the requested capabil
nodes.text: The capability agent used the same EKG to validate whether the requested capability concepts existed within the enterprise ontology.

nodes.label: We used the exact basic instructions for the entity agent and the prompt techniq
nodes.text: We used the exact basic instructions for the entity agent and the prompt technique for the capability agent, as we have used in Configuration 1.

nodes.label: The difference is the addition of an EKG.
nodes.text: The difference is the addition of an EKG.

nodes.label: Level Capability Parent Description Claims Management Ability to identify, track
nodes.text: Level Capability Parent Description Claims Management Ability to identify, track, and respond to a demand or request to indemnify customers under the terms of an agreement Claims Submission Claims Management Facilitating the process of customers submitting claims with the required documentation and information Claims Assistance Claims Submission Providing support to customers during the claim submission process to ensure completeness and compliance Claims Intake Claims Submission Collecting and logging claim information for initial review and assessment Claims Settlement Claims Management Managing the process of evaluating, negotiating, and resolving claims in accordance with policies Claims Evaluation Claims Settlement Assessing the validity and coverage of submitted claims against policy terms Claims Payment Claims Settlement Coordinate the disbursement of approved claim payments to customers In this configuration, we performed a matching test to verify whether the capabilities generated match those in EKG.

nodes.label: If a match was found, the agent retrieved the corresponding subgraph and used it
nodes.text: If a match was found, the agent retrieved the corresponding subgraph and used it to enhance or tailor the generated capability map.

nodes.label: If the ontology did not contain a matching concept, the system generated a respo
nodes.text: If the ontology did not contain a matching concept, the system generated a response indicating the absence of semantic alignment and optionally suggested alternative capabilities or taxonomic nodes.

nodes.label: Config 2 EKG-based BCM The extracted portion (claim management capability) of th
nodes.text: Config 2 EKG-based BCM The extracted portion (claim management capability) of the response from the complete capability map is shown in Table 2 Query: Generate a 5-level capability map for the insurance industry in Japan Table 2.

nodes.label: Response of EKG-based BCM Level Capability Parent Description Match with EKG Cla
nodes.text: Response of EKG-based BCM Level Capability Parent Description Match with EKG Claims Management "Handling the entire lifecycle of insurance claims." Yes Claims Filing Claims Management "Accepting and documenting initial claims from policyholders."

nodes.label: Claim Information Collection Claims Filing "Gathering necessary information and

nodes.text: Negotiation with Claimant Claims Settlement "Engaging with claimants to agree on settlement terms." No Payment Processing Claims Settlement "Processing payments to settle claims." No Summary of findings: Key takeaways from the scenario illustration are: • Configuration 1 performed better at creative, unconstrained generation of the capability map, but was semantically unverified. • Configuration 2 offered semantic alignment and potential ontology alignment of capabilities, but it is limited to level 1.

nodes.label: 5. Challenges and Future Work
nodes.text: 5. Challenges and Future Work

nodes.label: This research project proposes the development of a BA copilot that can automate
nodes.text: This research project proposes the development of a BA copilot that can automate BA modelling through agentic AI.

nodes.label: We present an illustrative scenario of the proposed method, showcasing the poten
nodes.text: We present an illustrative scenario of the proposed method, showcasing the potential of Agentic AI in automating business capability modelling through the proposed ABMA.

nodes.label: We observe that when EKG is not incorporated into the agent architecture (i.e.,
nodes.text: We observe that when EKG is not incorporated into the agent architecture (i.e., Configuration 1 using ToT-prompted agents), entity extraction can be completed using the user query; however, it lacks semantic precision due to the absence of external grounding.

nodes.label: Configuration 2 addressed this by integrating an EKG, grounding the entities usi
nodes.text: Configuration 2 addressed this by integrating an EKG, grounding the entities using organisational ontologies and improving the validity of generated output.

nodes.label: These observations suggest that ABMA’s modular architecture and agent orchestrat
nodes.text: These observations suggest that ABMA’s modular architecture and agent orchestration proved effective for scalable and context-aware capability mapping.

nodes.label: The use of LLMs combined with structured knowledge significantly improved the ac
nodes.text: The use of LLMs combined with structured knowledge significantly improved the accuracy and adaptability of generated BCMs.

nodes.label: The next step in our project is to evaluate and expand the illustrative scenario
nodes.text: The next step in our project is to evaluate and expand the illustrative scenario to different industries and other hierarchical levels of capability modelling (Levels 2-3), followed by a systematic evaluation of agent performance.

nodes.label: We plan to extend our implementation to additional BA artefact agents, aiming to
nodes.text: We plan to extend our implementation to additional BA artefact agents, aiming to develop a more intelligent and autonomous BA method.

nodes.label: The end goal of this project is to provide architecture that can be adapted by B
nodes.text: The end goal of this project is to provide architecture that can be adapted by BA experts who are interested in automating their modelling process, while incorporating context-aware reasoning that enables trust in the system.

nodes.text: Data intelligence Measuring tangible gains from AI The virtue of patience Chapter three: The infrastructure refit Diseconomies of scale Getting data right—and getting the right data The burden of housekeeping Practitioner perspectives Chapter four: Find your edge Mix and match Choosing models Chapter five: Guardrails and governance Centres of excellence: ‘forcing functions’ for co-ordination Calibrating humans and machines Conclusion This Economist Impact report, commissioned by Databricks, combines a global survey of 715 technical executives and 385 data and artificial intelligence (AI) technologists who work across the fields of data engineering, data science and enterprise architecture.
