nodes.label: The present trajectory of artificial intelligence (AI)— investment, adoption, an
nodes.text: The present trajectory of artificial intelligence (AI)— investment, adoption, and results—makes abundantly clear that the technology is going to become an integral part of every business, irrespective of industry.
nodes.author: Economist Impact

nodes.label: Just as certain is the fact that success in this new landscape will require busi
nodes.text: Just as certain is the fact that success in this new landscape will require business leaders to do far more than adopt the latest and greatest AI model.
nodes.author: Economist Impact

nodes.label: Winners in each industry will be those who take a holistic approach that encompa
nodes.text: Winners in each industry will be those who take a holistic approach that encompasses data management, security, governance, culture, and domain-specific expertise.
nodes.author: Economist Impact

nodes.label: All of these areas are covered in the findings from this Economist Impact report
nodes.text: All of these areas are covered in the findings from this Economist Impact report, which surveyed 1,100 executives and technologists across 19 countries and eight industries and interviewed another 28 C-level executives.
nodes.author: Economist Impact

nodes.label: As a result, it is packed with insights to help technology leaders and data and
nodes.text: As a result, it is packed with insights to help technology leaders and data and AI professionals navigate their own AI journeys.
nodes.author: Economist Impact

nodes.label: A key finding from this report is that companies overwhelmingly want to integrat
nodes.text: A key finding from this report is that companies overwhelmingly want to integrate generative AI models with their own data.
nodes.author: Economist Impact

nodes.label: That requires modernising their enterprise data platform—addressing the problem
nodes.text: That requires modernising their enterprise data platform—addressing the problem of fragmentation and advancing beyond the capabilities of a single large commercial model.
nodes.author: Economist Impact

nodes.label: The future also lies in AI systems and agents comprising multiple components, us
nodes.text: The future also lies in AI systems and agents comprising multiple components, using custom models, each optimised for specific tasks.
nodes.author: Economist Impact

nodes.label: This approach allows for greater accuracy, cost, performance and security—crucia
nodes.text: This approach allows for greater accuracy, cost, performance and security—crucial factors when dealing with enterprise-grade, AI-powered applications.
nodes.author: Economist Impact

nodes.label: This report also finds that democratisation is a big driver for AI.
nodes.text: This report also finds that democratisation is a big driver for AI.
nodes.author: Economist Impact

nodes.label: Organisations want to make data more accessible by a wider range of employees an
nodes.text: Organisations want to make data more accessible by a wider range of employees and look to empower their people to build and refine AI models that reflect their day-to-day business needs.
nodes.author: Economist Impact

nodes.label: Imagine being able to ask your data a simple question in English or your native
nodes.text: Imagine being able to ask your data a simple question in English or your native language, like "How is our European business doing on fiscal year-end targets?” and getting immediate, detailed answers that are certified as accurate, truly reflecting the nuances of your business.
nodes.author: Economist Impact

nodes.label: Security and governance are also essential in the new AI landscape.
nodes.text: Security and governance are also essential in the new AI landscape.
nodes.author: Economist Impact

nodes.label: As organisations rush to implement AI solutions, they grapple with how to protec
nodes.text: As organisations rush to implement AI solutions, they grapple with how to protect sensitive data and comply with evolving regulations.
nodes.author: Economist Impact

nodes.label: Sometimes, they move too fast, but in this world, it’s also often detrimental to
nodes.text: Sometimes, they move too fast, but in this world, it’s also often detrimental to move too slow.
nodes.author: Economist Impact

nodes.label: Putting robust security and governance processes in place is critically importan
nodes.text: Putting robust security and governance processes in place is critically important to unlocking pathways for innovation and not becoming paralysed by an overabundance of caution, particularly as the regulatory environment around AI evolves.
nodes.author: Economist Impact

nodes.label: All of the above means that the future lies not only with AI models trained on w
nodes.text: All of the above means that the future lies not only with AI models trained on web data, but with true data intelligence—ie, systems that deeply understand and operate on an organisation's specific, often sensitive data sets.
nodes.author: Economist Impact

nodes.label: AI that understands the context of your unique business.
nodes.text: AI that understands the context of your unique business.
nodes.author: Economist Impact

nodes.label: This approach—agent systems leveraging specialised models—allows companies to bu
nodes.text: This approach—agent systems leveraging specialised models—allows companies to build solutions tailored to their particular needs and domains, leveraging their proprietary data as a powerful competitive advantage.
nodes.author: Economist Impact

nodes.label: The AI revolution is just beginning, and one thing is certain: the organisations
nodes.text: The AI revolution is just beginning, and one thing is certain: the organisations that effectively harness their data and build AI systems tailored to their unique needs will be the leaders of tomorrow.
nodes.author: Economist Impact

nodes.label: At Databricks, we're proud to support research that helps illuminate the opportu
nodes.text: At Databricks, we're proud to support research that helps illuminate the opportunities and strategies that will define the AI era.
nodes.author: Economist Impact

nodes.label: Andy Kofoid President of global field operations, Databricks A heady US$1trn in
nodes.text: Andy Kofoid President of global field operations, Databricks A heady US$1trn in capital expenditure is expected in the coming years to deliver the data centres, chips, energy and infrastructure to support the artificial intelligence (AI)
nodes.author: Economist Impact

nodes.label: revolution.1 While this figure is largely driven by the dawn of generative AI (G
nodes.text: revolution.1 While this figure is largely driven by the dawn of generative AI (GenAI), the truth is that companies have been developing their AI capabilities for years.
nodes.author: Economist Impact

nodes.label: Breakthroughs like deep learning and neural networks have allowed specialists in
nodes.text: Breakthroughs like deep learning and neural networks have allowed specialists in fields from biotech to finance to crunch vast datasets to uncover patterns and deliver actionable intelligence.
nodes.author: Economist Impact

nodes.label: The difference now is democratisation and scale.
nodes.text: The difference now is democratisation and scale.
nodes.author: Economist Impact

nodes.label: Because GenAI provides an intuitive, natural language interface, the benefits of
nodes.text: Because GenAI provides an intuitive, natural language interface, the benefits of AI have become accessible to every practitioner.
nodes.author: Economist Impact

nodes.label: To transition successfully from pilots to enterprise-wide deployment requires a
nodes.text: To transition successfully from pilots to enterprise-wide deployment requires a robust infrastructure that can handle the data and computational requirements of AI, a workforce strategy that appropriately calibrates humans versus machines, and an appropriate return on investment (ROI) strategy.
nodes.author: Economist Impact

nodes.label: Last, but not least, achieving AI excellence necessitates strong governance and
nodes.text: Last, but not least, achieving AI excellence necessitates strong governance and careful design of human machine interaction.
nodes.author: Economist Impact

nodes.label: Decisions and outputs must comply with extensive existing and emerging legislati
nodes.text: Decisions and outputs must comply with extensive existing and emerging legislation in areas like data privacy, security and consumer protection.
nodes.author: Economist Impact

nodes.label: This Economist Impact report, commissioned by Databricks, combines a global surv
nodes.text: This Economist Impact report, commissioned by Databricks, combines a global survey of 715 technical executives and 385 data and AI technologists who work across the fields of data engineering, data science and enterprise architecture.
nodes.author: Economist Impact

nodes.label: It also features insights from interviews with 28 C-suite executives from leadin
nodes.text: It also features insights from interviews with 28 C-suite executives from leading organisations across ten countries, who represent 11 industries.
nodes.author: Economist Impact

nodes.label: Blending survey trends with qualitative insights, this report provides a compreh
nodes.text: Blending survey trends with qualitative insights, this report provides a comprehensive assessment of AI implementation from both executives and practitioners’ points of view, revealing catalysts and bottlenecks and identifying strategies for success.
nodes.author: Economist Impact

nodes.label: Goldman Sachs, “Gen AI: too much spend, too little benefit?”, 2024, https://www.
nodes.text: Goldman Sachs, “Gen AI: too much spend, too little benefit?”, 2024, https://www.goldmansachs.com/insights/top-of-mind/gen-ai-too-much-spend- too-little-benefit
nodes.author: Economist Impact

nodes.label: Executives and practitioners alike believe in the power of AI, but think investm
nodes.text: Executives and practitioners alike believe in the power of AI, but think investment
nodes.author: Economist Impact

nodes.label: is falling short.
nodes.text: is falling short.
nodes.author: Economist Impact

nodes.label: From accelerating drug development to extending credit to the financially exclud
nodes.text: From accelerating drug development to extending credit to the financially excluded, companies are finding a diverse array of applications for AI and GenAI.
nodes.author: Economist Impact

nodes.label: Our survey found that 85% of organisations are actively using GenAI in at least
nodes.text: Our survey found that 85% of organisations are actively using GenAI in at least one business function, reaching 97% of companies with revenue over US$10bn.
nodes.author: Economist Impact

nodes.label: IT teams are the most avid adopters and the legal function is the most reticent.
nodes.text: IT teams are the most avid adopters and the legal function is the most reticent.
nodes.author: Economist Impact

nodes.label: Internal projects are preferred but, by 2027, 99% of executives expect GenAI ado
nodes.text: Internal projects are preferred but, by 2027, 99% of executives expect GenAI adoption across internal and external use cases.
nodes.author: Economist Impact

nodes.label: Seven in ten see AI as crucial to their long-term strategic goals, and only 18%
nodes.text: Seven in ten see AI as crucial to their long-term strategic goals, and only 18% say it is overhyped.
nodes.author: Economist Impact

nodes.label: Despite the momentum, only one in five believe that their current investment acr
nodes.text: Despite the momentum, only one in five believe that their current investment across technical and non-technical domains is sufficient.
nodes.author: Economist Impact

nodes.label: AI’s short-term benefits include productivity and efficiency, but leaders will u
nodes.text: AI’s short-term benefits include productivity and efficiency, but leaders will use AI to unlock value, revenue and business model innovation in the long term. So far, productivity
nodes.author: Economist Impact

nodes.label: is one of the most commonly reported impacts of AI.
nodes.text: is one of the most commonly reported impacts of AI.
nodes.author: Economist Impact

nodes.label: In line with this, functions with high automation potential have been heavily te
nodes.text: In line with this, functions with high automation potential have been heavily testing GenAI, such as IT (91%), marketing (85%), sales and customer service (83%), and operations (80%), while 82% of data scientists report using AI for coding.
nodes.author: Economist Impact

nodes.label: Interviewees also report a range of benefits, from cost reduction to improved em
nodes.text: Interviewees also report a range of benefits, from cost reduction to improved employee experience and talent attraction.
nodes.author: Economist Impact

nodes.label: However, executives do take a ‘long’ view of the GenAI paradigm shift, noting th
nodes.text: However, executives do take a ‘long’ view of the GenAI paradigm shift, noting that strategic considerations like business model innovation, market positioning, and environment, social and governance standards have been the most important elements when evaluating business cases for AI.
nodes.author: Economist Impact

nodes.label: Revenue growth has been the least effective metric for justifying investment to
nodes.text: Revenue growth has been the least effective metric for justifying investment to date, with experts arguing that AI returns will take time to accrue given the need for experimentation, iteration and digital infrastructure overhauls.
nodes.author: Economist Impact

nodes.label: Over time, however, the ability to realise revenue will set leaders apart, and f
nodes.text: Over time, however, the ability to realise revenue will set leaders apart, and financial value will need to be more definitively quantified.
nodes.author: Economist Impact

nodes.label: Enterprise-wide AI requires an infrastructure refit—most companies do not feel c
nodes.text: Enterprise-wide AI requires an infrastructure refit—most companies do not feel confident in their current architecture. Many enterprises
nodes.author: Economist Impact

nodes.label: are operating with the technological equivalent of Victorian-era plumbing.
nodes.text: are operating with the technological equivalent of Victorian-era plumbing.
nodes.author: Economist Impact

nodes.label: Only 22% of organisations say their current architecture can support AI workload
nodes.text: Only 22% of organisations say their current architecture can support AI workloads without modifications, and 48% of data engineers spend most of their time resolving data source connections.
nodes.author: Economist Impact

nodes.label: The problem could worsen as democratisation leads to a proliferation of AI pilot
nodes.text: The problem could worsen as democratisation leads to a proliferation of AI pilots, leading to congestion, complexity, and opacity in data and infrastructure.
nodes.author: Economist Impact

nodes.label: The prize is worth the work; some of the companies interviewed for this programm
nodes.text: The prize is worth the work; some of the companies interviewed for this programme reported that they could be truly creative in finding use cases and achieving returns only once they had secured their data foundation.
nodes.author: Economist Impact

nodes.label: Worries about data security, from silos to fragmented systems, are holding some
nodes.text: Worries about data security, from silos to fragmented systems, are holding some companies back from more ambitious experimentation.
nodes.author: Economist Impact

nodes.label: Two-thirds of organisations see significant potential in integrating GenAI model
nodes.text: Two-thirds of organisations see significant potential in integrating GenAI models with their own data. As AI models become
nodes.author: Economist Impact

nodes.label: commoditised, the best performers will mix and match models and tools with their
nodes.text: commoditised, the best performers will mix and match models and tools with their unique assets: proprietary data and know-how.
nodes.author: Economist Impact

nodes.label: This requires finding the right blend of tools, such as open- and closed-source
nodes.text: This requires finding the right blend of tools, such as open- and closed-source models, and gaining visibility and mastery over internal datasets.
nodes.author: Economist Impact

nodes.label: Organisations are exploring a variety of models, and nearly seven out of ten are
nodes.text: Organisations are exploring a variety of models, and nearly seven out of ten are experimenting with or have fully deployed open-source GenAI, with 96% believing they will do so by 2027.
nodes.author: Economist Impact

nodes.label: Experts warn that off-the-shelf models are inferior in areas like domain-specifi
nodes.text: Experts warn that off-the-shelf models are inferior in areas like domain-specific lexicon, and may provide less control and security.
nodes.author: Economist Impact

nodes.label: However, off-the-shelf models, unconnected to enterprise data, are still common:
nodes.text: However, off-the-shelf models, unconnected to enterprise data, are still common: almost half (45%) of data scientists are using large language models (LLMs) without retrieval augmented generation (RAG).
nodes.author: Economist Impact

nodes.label: Few enterprises have fully ‘productionised’ GenAI due to cost, skills and govern
nodes.text: Few enterprises have fully ‘productionised’ GenAI due to cost, skills and governance
nodes.author: Economist Impact

nodes.label: challenges.
nodes.text: challenges.
nodes.author: Economist Impact

nodes.label: Only 37% of executives believe their GenAI applications are production-ready, a
nodes.text: Only 37% of executives believe their GenAI applications are production-ready, a figure that falls to just 29% among practitioners.
nodes.author: Economist Impact

nodes.label: Data scientists cite key constraints including cost (41%), skills (40%), quality
nodes.text: Data scientists cite key constraints including cost (41%), skills (40%), quality (37%) and governance (33%).
nodes.author: Economist Impact

nodes.label: Only one in six respondents believe that their organisation can secure enough AI
nodes.text: Only one in six respondents believe that their organisation can secure enough AI talent, half of data engineers say governance takes up more time than anything else, and more than half of enterprise architects (53%) cite challenges with data privacy and security breaches as the biggest risk of AI expansion.
nodes.author: Economist Impact

nodes.label: Executives agree that in the years ahead, winners will be those that graduate fr
nodes.text: Executives agree that in the years ahead, winners will be those that graduate from experiments to production, scaling and monetisation.
nodes.author: Economist Impact

nodes.label: The inevitability of mass AI adoption underscores the critical need to carefully
nodes.text: The inevitability of mass AI adoption underscores the critical need to carefully calibrate human and machine intelligence to enable democratised AI ecosystems.
nodes.author: Economist Impact

nodes.label: Leaders say AI can augment rather than replace employees.
nodes.text: Leaders say AI can augment rather than replace employees.
nodes.author: Economist Impact

nodes.label: Interviewees cite unified systems and ‘centres of excellence’ as critical to bal
nodes.text: Interviewees cite unified systems and ‘centres of excellence’ as critical to balancing governance and enablement, pointing to an irreplaceable role for experience and judgement in overseeing AI outputs.
nodes.author: Economist Impact

nodes.label: Two-thirds of organisations say they are still experimenting to find the right b
nodes.text: Two-thirds of organisations say they are still experimenting to find the right balance between humans and AI.
nodes.author: Economist Impact

nodes.label: Many are also investing in self-service tools and AI assistants to empower all e
nodes.text: Many are also investing in self-service tools and AI assistants to empower all employees to become data scientists.
nodes.author: Economist Impact

nodes.label: Enterprise architects see GenAI as a democratising force, with nearly 60% predic
nodes.text: Enterprise architects see GenAI as a democratising force, with nearly 60% predicting that within three years natural language will be the primary—if not only—way that non- technical staff interact with complex datasets.
nodes.author: Economist Impact

nodes.label: Organisations are racing to harness the power of AI, with generative models elev
nodes.text: Organisations are racing to harness the power of AI, with generative models elevating capabilities across industries and functions.
nodes.author: Economist Impact

nodes.label: From enhancing drug discovery to powering intelligent assistants, a range of mod
nodes.text: From enhancing drug discovery to powering intelligent assistants, a range of models and techniques are boosting productivity, guiding strategic decisions, and opening new frontiers of innovation.
nodes.author: Economist Impact

nodes.label: As companies move from experimentation and implementation, the future belongs to
nodes.text: As companies move from experimentation and implementation, the future belongs to those who can successfully scale AI solutions.
nodes.author: Economist Impact

nodes.label: Two years since the public launch of ChatGPT, every organisation is searching fo
nodes.text: Two years since the public launch of ChatGPT, every organisation is searching for algorithmic advantage.
nodes.author: Economist Impact

nodes.label: Companies are harnessing the powers of so-called traditional AI—which can recogn
nodes.text: Companies are harnessing the powers of so-called traditional AI—which can recognise patterns, process vast and varied data sets, and make predictions based on past data—with the ability of GenAI to reason and converse across contexts, creating novel outputs that mimic human ability from natural language prompts.2 The GenAI breakthrough took AI from narrow tasks performed by expert teams to a capability available to nearly everyone. “Gen AI has exploded because of its approachability; the average person can use these tools,” says Darrin Vohs, global chief information officer at Molson Coors, a Canadian beverage company.
nodes.author: Economist Impact

nodes.label: Many companies were already experimenting with AI long before ChatGPT was releas
nodes.text: Many companies were already experimenting with AI long before ChatGPT was released, especially larger enterprises in more technically advanced fields.
nodes.author: Economist Impact

nodes.label: Novartis, a Swiss pharmaceutical giant, had been using machine learning (ML) for
nodes.text: Novartis, a Swiss pharmaceutical giant, had been using machine learning (ML) for drug discovery and predictive analytics to improve clinical trials.3,4 Flo Health, a global consumer-facing women’s health app used by over 380 million people, is based on an algorithm using traditional ML techniques. “Advancements in LLMs have been really exciting for us,” says Andy Hill, chief data officer at Unilever, a consumer goods firm. “But it's still the combination of that and the more traditional supervised learning techniques that together will provide the value for a company like us.” MIT News | Massachusetts Institute of Technology, “Explained: Generative AI”, 2023, https://news.mit.edu/2023/explained-generative-ai-1109 Novartis, “The art of drug design in a technological age”, 2021, https://www.novartis.com/stories/art-drug-design-technological-age Novartis, “Novartis' commitment to the ethical and responsible use of Artificial Intelligence (AI) Systems”, 2020, https://www.novartis.com/sites/novartis_com/files/novartis-responsible-use-of-ai-systems.pdf Generative models elevate AI capabilities due to their broad applications.
nodes.author: Economist Impact

nodes.label: Even those with ample AI experience have added a new layer of capability.
nodes.text: Even those with ample AI experience have added a new layer of capability.
nodes.author: Economist Impact

nodes.label: Thermo Fisher Scientific, a global life sciences company, has been incorporating
nodes.text: Thermo Fisher Scientific, a global life sciences company, has been incorporating AI into its instruments for nearly a decade.
nodes.author: Economist Impact

nodes.label: GenAI builds on this strong AI/ML foundation, according to Ryan Snyder, the comp
nodes.text: GenAI builds on this strong AI/ML foundation, according to Ryan Snyder, the company’s chief information officer, enabling deeper interrogation of data and bolder experimentation.
nodes.author: Economist Impact

nodes.label: While AI has been through its share of winters, our survey finds respondents are
nodes.text: While AI has been through its share of winters, our survey finds respondents are overwhelmingly optimistic, with 73% saying GenAI is critical to their long-term strategic goals.
nodes.author: Economist Impact

nodes.label: Only 18% say AI is overhyped, although executives are slightly more cautious (20
nodes.text: Only 18% say AI is overhyped, although executives are slightly more cautious (20% lean towards there being too much hype) than data and AI technologists (just 13%).
nodes.author: Economist Impact

nodes.label: In the years ahead, winners and losers will be defined by who is able to graduat
nodes.text: In the years ahead, winners and losers will be defined by who is able to graduate from experiments to implementation at scale.
nodes.author: Economist Impact

nodes.label: Amid this cautious optimism, the rapid adoption of GenAI is hard to ignore.
nodes.text: Amid this cautious optimism, the rapid adoption of GenAI is hard to ignore.
nodes.author: Economist Impact

nodes.label: An impressive 85% of organisations in our survey are testing and using it today,
nodes.text: An impressive 85% of organisations in our survey are testing and using it today, with adoption rates climbing as company size increases—among those with revenue over US$10bn, 97% are testing and using it.
nodes.author: Economist Impact

nodes.label: This trend suggests that larger organisations may cement their competitive advan
nodes.text: This trend suggests that larger organisations may cement their competitive advantages through AI, while those lagging in investment may miss out on the next major technology shift. “There’s always some truth underneath the hype.
nodes.author: Economist Impact

nodes.label: When the dot-com boom happened, lots of companies failed, but a lot of real comp
nodes.text: When the dot-com boom happened, lots of companies failed, but a lot of real companies with valuable products came out of it as well, and I believe the same is going to happen in the GenAI space,” predicts Mr Vohs at Molson Coors. “GenAI is going to be a commodity embedded in everything, similarly to the internet, and the value will be in the business problems it helps solve.”
nodes.author: Economist Impact

nodes.label: “GenAI is going to be a commodity embedded in everything, similarly to the inter
nodes.text: “GenAI is going to be a commodity embedded in everything, similarly to the internet, and the value will be in the business problems it helps solve.”
nodes.author: Economist Impact

nodes.label: Darrin Vohs, global chief information officer, Molson Coors To date, GenAI usage
nodes.text: Darrin Vohs, global chief information officer, Molson Coors To date, GenAI usage is most advanced among IT teams, our survey found, with 62% having fully deployed it.
nodes.author: Economist Impact

nodes.label: Marketing and sales, including customer service, are leading adoption ahead of o
nodes.text: Marketing and sales, including customer service, are leading adoption ahead of other business teams (see figure 1).
nodes.author: Economist Impact

nodes.label: Companies interviewed for this study report quantifiable productivity gains amon
nodes.text: Companies interviewed for this study report quantifiable productivity gains among tech teams in particular.
nodes.author: Economist Impact

nodes.label: Flo Health has seen a 24% boost in data engineering efficiency, with AI assistan
nodes.text: Flo Health has seen a 24% boost in data engineering efficiency, with AI assistants reducing mistakes and accelerating document generation.
nodes.author: Economist Impact

nodes.label: Repsol, a Spanish multinational energy company, ran a GenAI experiment with 200
nodes.text: Repsol, a Spanish multinational energy company, ran a GenAI experiment with 200 coders and more than 200,000 lines of code across 20 programming languages, finding improvements in productivity of up to 30%, with an average of 7%, depending on the technology.
nodes.author: Economist Impact

nodes.label: The quality of output is high too.
nodes.text: The quality of output is high too.
nodes.author: Economist Impact

nodes.label: Japanese technology company Rakuten has also reported that a significant number
nodes.text: Japanese technology company Rakuten has also reported that a significant number of coding outputs from its internal AI tool were accepted without modification.
nodes.author: Economist Impact

nodes.label: While those in tech roles like programming may find that GenAI is a natural fit,
nodes.text: While those in tech roles like programming may find that GenAI is a natural fit, the idea of an assistant to everyone has increasing appeal.
nodes.author: Economist Impact

nodes.label: ‘Agentic AI’—artificial agents with a natural language interface that can plan a
nodes.text: ‘Agentic AI’—artificial agents with a natural language interface that can plan and execute sequences of actions on behalf of a user—will become an increasingly common feature of daily life, surpassing text-to-speech applications like Alexa and Siri in scope and smarts.
nodes.author: Economist Impact

nodes.label: They could be holiday bookers, counsellors and creative sparring partners, or ne
nodes.text: They could be holiday bookers, counsellors and creative sparring partners, or negotiate legal disputes and take on unscrupulous corporate practices.5 Our global survey finds that more than 97% of enterprise architects predict that non-technical staff will use natural language programmes to interact with complex datasets within the next three years.
nodes.author: Economist Impact

nodes.label: Nearly six in ten (58%) say that natural language will be either the primary or
nodes.text: Nearly six in ten (58%) say that natural language will be either the primary or only way they do this.
nodes.author: Economist Impact

nodes.label: The majority of business functions in our survey have either fully deployed or a
nodes.text: The majority of business functions in our survey have either fully deployed or are actively experimenting with GenAI (see figure 1).
nodes.author: Economist Impact

nodes.label: The most hesitant in-house functions are legal and finance, the only divisions i
nodes.text: The most hesitant in-house functions are legal and finance, the only divisions in our survey with less than half of respondents in full deployment or experimentation.
nodes.author: Economist Impact

nodes.label: That caution may be warranted given regulatory constraints and high stakes, alth
nodes.text: That caution may be warranted given regulatory constraints and high stakes, although plenty of law firms are trying to embrace AI tools for use cases like drafting and reviewing contracts.
nodes.author: Economist Impact

nodes.label: Figure 1: Stage of GenAI adoption by business function
nodes.text: Figure 1: Stage of GenAI adoption by business function
nodes.author: Economist Impact

nodes.label: Not using now and not planning to use in the next three years Not using now but
nodes.text: Not using now and not planning to use in the next three years Not using now but planning to use in the next three years Experimenting/ piloting Currently using in production (ie, fully deployed)
nodes.author: Economist Impact

nodes.label: R&D or product development Sales and customer service Operations and supply chai
nodes.text: R&D or product development Sales and customer service Operations and supply chain For example: https://donotpay.com/ In contrast to the trailing status of individual finance teams, financial services leads all other industries in GenAI implementation.
nodes.author: Economist Impact

nodes.label: For these firms, 45% are already expanding and scaling production of use cases a
nodes.text: For these firms, 45% are already expanding and scaling production of use cases and another 36% are in active deployment (see figure 2).
nodes.author: Economist Impact

nodes.label: Early adopters are also common across the telecoms, retail and manufacturing sec
nodes.text: Early adopters are also common across the telecoms, retail and manufacturing sectors, suggesting significant potential for growth as they fine-tune their approaches.
nodes.author: Economist Impact

nodes.label: Finally, media and entertainment and the public sector currently lag in terms of
nodes.text: Finally, media and entertainment and the public sector currently lag in terms of scaled-up use cases, but the majority of organisations in those sectors are still beginning to actively deploy GenAI in production.
nodes.author: Economist Impact

nodes.label: In most countries, about 70% of organisations have moved past the piloting stage
nodes.text: In most countries, about 70% of organisations have moved past the piloting stage for GenAI (see figure 3). However, progress is much more varied when it comes to the scaling up stage.
nodes.author: Economist Impact

nodes.label: In India, for instance, most companies (56%) are scaling up GenAI, relying on pu
nodes.text: In India, for instance, most companies (56%) are scaling up GenAI, relying on public and open-source models much more than organisations in other countries.
nodes.author: Economist Impact

nodes.label: Meanwhile, in Japan just 6% are scaling up, which may reflect the country’s pref
nodes.text: Meanwhile, in Japan just 6% are scaling up, which may reflect the country’s preference for more complex, customised models. Half of Japanese executives said they were training custom models based on proprietary data, a rate far beyond any other country.
nodes.author: Economist Impact

nodes.label: Similarly, for the US, which has the highest share of organisations still in the
nodes.text: Similarly, for the US, which has the highest share of organisations still in the piloting stage, this perceived lag may not actually represent a weakness.
nodes.author: Economist Impact

nodes.label: Our data suggest that many of these experimenters may in fact be ‘GenAI leaders’
nodes.text: Our data suggest that many of these experimenters may in fact be ‘GenAI leaders’— that is, they are also the most confident about the readiness of their GenAI applications.
nodes.author: Economist Impact

nodes.label: This is also true of firms in Europe to a lesser extent.
nodes.text: This is also true of firms in Europe to a lesser extent.
nodes.author: Economist Impact

nodes.label: These findings warrant further investigation, but it may be the case that leadin
nodes.text: These findings warrant further investigation, but it may be the case that leading companies in the US are more willing to take risks and explore more innovative use cases rather than rushing to scale up basic ‘tried and tested’ use cases.
nodes.author: Economist Impact

nodes.label: Figure 2: Stage of GenAI implementation by industry
nodes.text: Figure 2: Stage of GenAI implementation by industry
nodes.author: Economist Impact

nodes.label: No activity Exploring/piloting Deployment and productionising Expanding and scal
nodes.text: No activity Exploring/piloting Deployment and productionising Expanding and scaling production Financial services, banking and insurance Healthcare, pharmaceuticals and life sciences Energy, oil and gas (including utilities)
nodes.author: Economist Impact

nodes.label: Government agencies Media and entertainment Telecommunications Retail and consum
nodes.text: Government agencies Media and entertainment Telecommunications Retail and consumer goods Manufacturing (including automotive)
nodes.author: Economist Impact

nodes.label: Productivity is an understandable initial focus because it’s measurable—and it m
nodes.text: Productivity is an understandable initial focus because it’s measurable—and it matters.
nodes.author: Economist Impact

nodes.label: But executives are using both classical AI/ML and GenAI to guide strategic decis
nodes.text: But executives are using both classical AI/ML and GenAI to guide strategic decision-making in a volatile operating environment.
nodes.author: Economist Impact

nodes.label: Our survey found risk management was a dominant strategic area in which AI as a
nodes.text: Our survey found risk management was a dominant strategic area in which AI as a whole is informing decisions at companies of all sizes, reported by 68%, followed by market entry (66%), and strategic sales and partnerships (66%).
nodes.author: Economist Impact

nodes.label: By contrast, larger companies are more likely to use AI for strategic decisions
nodes.text: By contrast, larger companies are more likely to use AI for strategic decisions around product development (67%), hiring and workforce planning (67%), and investment and capital allocation (61%) compared with mid-size companies (55%, 53% and 48%, respectively).
nodes.author: Economist Impact

nodes.label: The value of AI for uncovering hidden insights is clear for many large enterpris
nodes.text: The value of AI for uncovering hidden insights is clear for many large enterprises, such as Mahindra Group, an Indian multinational conglomerate. “We have many listed companies,” says Mohit Kapoor, the group chief technology officer. “We ensure data is always identifiable so that it can be segregated, but we can also anonymise that data to drive insights from across our companies.” For firms operating in an increasingly unpredictable world, AI provides armour in the form of resilience and informs better decisions.
nodes.author: Economist Impact

nodes.label: UPS, a multinational shipping company, uses GenAI to help businesses across its
nodes.text: UPS, a multinational shipping company, uses GenAI to help businesses across its 200 countries and territories on issues like understanding customs codes for products.6 “When we infuse decision science into software, we remove some of the noise and inconsistency with decision- making throughout a large worldwide company like ours,” says Ken Finnerty, president of IT and data analytics at UPS. “We ensure consistency and raise quality.
nodes.author: Economist Impact

nodes.label: Delivering fast decisions, but more importantly the right decisions.”
nodes.text: Delivering fast decisions, but more importantly the right decisions.”
nodes.author: Economist Impact

nodes.label: Figure 3: Stage of GenAI implementation by country/region
nodes.text: Figure 3: Stage of GenAI implementation by country/region
nodes.author: Economist Impact

nodes.label: Exploring/piloting No activity Deployment and productionising Expanding and scal
nodes.text: Exploring/piloting No activity Deployment and productionising Expanding and scaling production South Korea Other Europe Mastercard, “Mastercard accelerates card fraud detection with generative AI technology”, 2024, https://www.mastercard.com/news/press/2024/may/mastercard-accelerates-card-fraud-detection-with-generative-ai- technology/ JetBlue, a US airline, has developed digital twins— ML-powered replicas of physical systems—to enhance predictive capabilities in an industry rife with unexpected disruptions from weather events to technical issues. “In the airline business, the number of unexpected things that can come your way in a given day is almost limitless, so our digital twin is becoming an incredible source of value for anticipating events and preparing solutions,” says Carol Clements, JetBlue’s chief digital and technology officer.
nodes.author: Economist Impact

nodes.label: This has improved operational efficiency and minimised disruption costs.
nodes.text: This has improved operational efficiency and minimised disruption costs.
nodes.author: Economist Impact

nodes.label: Mastercard uses GenAI to enhance and speed up card fraud detection. “We’re scann
nodes.text: Mastercard uses GenAI to enhance and speed up card fraud detection. “We’re scanning a trillion data points globally to predict whether a transaction is genuine or not, and we continue to improve the capabilities as we add new technologies,” says Greg Ulrich, the company’s chief AI and data officer. “You can never determine that a transaction is fraudulent with 100% certainty, but we’re trying to give banks the confidence to make an informed decision.” General Motors (GM) uses ML to analyse diagnostic data to predict issues before they become serious: the software can anticipate if a vehicle battery on the production line will have problems, for instance, so it can be removed and fixed before being installed in a car. “It helps from a customer loyalty perspective as well, by averting a customer having a bad experience with their first EV [electric vehicle], which could erode trust in the brand,” says Jon Francis, the automaker’s chief data and analytics officer.
nodes.author: Economist Impact

nodes.label: AI can also guide decisions that let companies expand their customer base withou
nodes.text: AI can also guide decisions that let companies expand their customer base without taking undue risk.
nodes.author: Economist Impact

nodes.label: Mahindra Group uses AI to analyse publicly available and proprietary data to mak
nodes.text: Mahindra Group uses AI to analyse publicly available and proprietary data to make lending decisions, creating credit scores that could promote financial inclusion.
nodes.author: Economist Impact

nodes.label: Siam Commercial Bank, a Thailand-based bank, used ML to reduce credit underwriti
nodes.text: Siam Commercial Bank, a Thailand-based bank, used ML to reduce credit underwriting costs by 20%, says Chalee Asavathiratham, the former chief digital banking officer. “Applying for a loan becomes unlocking, if you will, a pre-calculated credit number.
nodes.author: Economist Impact

nodes.label: So the loan process becomes much more streamlined, much easier.” Unilever is emb
nodes.text: So the loan process becomes much more streamlined, much easier.” Unilever is embedding AI-driven intelligence into its decision-making process.
nodes.author: Economist Impact

nodes.label: Through the Horizon3 Lab, it is focusing on identifying and testing new AI conce
nodes.text: Through the Horizon3 Lab, it is focusing on identifying and testing new AI concepts, designs and projects.7 Its Sky programme is leveraging advanced analytics to optimise its product portfolio to make faster, smarter decisions about what it puts on the shelves, explains Mr Hill. “I think we’ve made really significant progress towards being a data intelligent company, and there is still a huge opportunity ahead.” “We’re scanning a trillion data points globally to predict whether a transaction is genuine or not, and we continue to improve the capabilities as we add new technologies.
nodes.author: Economist Impact

nodes.label: You can never determine that a transaction is fraudulent with 100% certainty, bu
nodes.text: You can never determine that a transaction is fraudulent with 100% certainty, but we’re trying to give banks the confidence to make an informed decision.” Greg Ulrich, chief AI and data officer, Mastercard
nodes.author: Economist Impact

nodes.label: “I think we’ve made really
nodes.text: “I think we’ve made really
nodes.author: Economist Impact

nodes.label: significant progress towards being a data intelligent company, and there is stil
nodes.text: significant progress towards being a data intelligent company, and there is still a huge opportunity ahead.”
nodes.author: Economist Impact

nodes.label: Andy Hill, chief data officer, Unilever Unilever, “Unilever Launches Global AI L
nodes.text: Andy Hill, chief data officer, Unilever Unilever, “Unilever Launches Global AI Lab in Toronto”, 2023, https://www.unilever.ca/news/press-releases/2023/unilever-launches-global-ai-lab-in-toronto/ Whether in productivity or in prototyping, companies are picking their pilots carefully.
nodes.author: Economist Impact

nodes.label: Sara Vaezy, chief strategy and digital officer for Providence, a healthcare orga
nodes.text: Sara Vaezy, chief strategy and digital officer for Providence, a healthcare organisation based in the US, explains its approach of being “very methodical and very intentional” to avoid the mindset of “spray and pray”.
nodes.author: Economist Impact

nodes.label: For its approach, she explains, “we set up a governance structure, we set up a t
nodes.text: For its approach, she explains, “we set up a governance structure, we set up a technical environment where we could do these experiments very methodically and intentionally and instrument [them] properly, so we could understand what was happening.” Many companies prefer internal-facing applications before releasing products to customers.
nodes.author: Economist Impact

nodes.label: TD Bank Group, a Canadian multinational financial institution, for instance, has
nodes.text: TD Bank Group, a Canadian multinational financial institution, for instance, has deployed a GenAI chatbot for its call centre staff but has kept it internal.
nodes.author: Economist Impact

nodes.label: Firms are understandably cautious about releasing chatbots directly to customers
nodes.text: Firms are understandably cautious about releasing chatbots directly to customers.
nodes.author: Economist Impact

nodes.label: While they might offer speed and efficiency, unpredictability can lead to offens
nodes.text: While they might offer speed and efficiency, unpredictability can lead to offensive content and misinformation, damaging both consumer trust and corporate reputations.
nodes.author: Economist Impact

nodes.label: Consumer perceptions are one consideration for firms looking to external use cas
nodes.text: Consumer perceptions are one consideration for firms looking to external use cases, to avoid convenience sliding into lack of control.
nodes.author: Economist Impact

nodes.label: Users may grow wary when AI feels invasive or manipulative, raising worries abou
nodes.text: Users may grow wary when AI feels invasive or manipulative, raising worries about privacy risks,8 or when it offers erratic recommendations, like telling users to eat rocks and glue cheese to pizza.9 Rogue bots have even been gamed to promote competitors.10 “With information that a user might act on, such as ‘what could I learn about a symptom I’m experiencing?’, there is undoubtedly a need for more caution,” says Roman Bugaev, chief technology officer at Flo Health.
nodes.author: Economist Impact

nodes.label: The US Department of Defense (DoD) draws a line between administrative and battl
nodes.text: The US Department of Defense (DoD) draws a line between administrative and battlefield applications, with far higher stakes associated with decision support in warfare. “It’s one thing using an LLM on the administrative side, to process DoD regulations to make sure your documents meet them.
nodes.author: Economist Impact

nodes.label: But decision support in warfare, like identifying a bad guy to shoot, is higher
nodes.text: But decision support in warfare, like identifying a bad guy to shoot, is higher stakes,” says Leonel Garciga, chief information officer for the US Army.
nodes.author: Economist Impact

nodes.label: Decision support will still remain with the respective commander, and humans wil
nodes.text: Decision support will still remain with the respective commander, and humans will always be in the loop as far as AI is involved.
nodes.author: Economist Impact

nodes.label: Internal experiments are a safe testing ground. “If you introduce products witho
nodes.text: Internal experiments are a safe testing ground. “If you introduce products without high reliability, that may erode customer trust,” argues Wassym Bensaid, chief software officer at Rivian, an EV manufacturer. “Launching a product requires a steep maturation climb from demonstration software.
nodes.author: Economist Impact

nodes.label: We only introduce products once we’ve addressed all the safeguards from a qualit
nodes.text: We only introduce products once we’ve addressed all the safeguards from a quality and reliability standpoint to ensure the technology will be a trusted assistant for our customers.” “We set up a governance structure, we set up a technical environment where we could do these experiments very methodically and intentionally and instrument [them] properly, so we could understand what was happening.” Sara Vaezy, chief strategy and digital officer, Providence BBC, “Snapchat’s My AI chatbot: The privacy concerns”, 2023, https://www.bbc.co.uk/news/technology-67027282 BBC, “Glue pizza and eat rocks: Google AI search errors go viral”, 2024, https://www.bbc.co.uk/news/articles/cd11gzejgz4o 10 Carscoops, “Chevy Dealers’ ChatGPT Bots Recommend Teslas, BMWs, Fords, Toyotas And Rivians” 2023, https://www.carscoops.com/2023/12/chevy-dealers-ai-chatbots-are-recommending-teslas-bmws-fords-toyotas-and- rivians/
nodes.author: Economist Impact

nodes.label: Borrowing from the software industry, many companies are looking to short sprint
nodes.text: Borrowing from the software industry, many companies are looking to short sprints and iteration as they work through AI’s prospects and pitfalls. “There’s no right answer, there’s going to always be multiple answers ... start small, iterate, learn quickly, fail, get feedback, and keep moving forward,” advises Melissa Pint, chief digital information officer at Frontier, a telecoms company.
nodes.author: Economist Impact

nodes.label: This careful strategy allows firms to explore potential without overextending. “
nodes.text: This careful strategy allows firms to explore potential without overextending. “Fewer than 20% of AI pilots end up in production,” says Gereurd Roberts, group managing director at Seven Digital, part of Australian media company Seven West Media. “It requires a heap of experimentation in working out what’s going to create value.” As Kushal Chakrabarti, chief data officer at Opendoor, an online real-estate company, notes, “if you can get a low precision prototype in a short amount of time, you can use it to answer some questions and think about the evolution, and then you can add precision and reliability over time.
nodes.author: Economist Impact

nodes.label: It’s a stronger version of working backwards.” The key, say experts, is to avoid
nodes.text: It’s a stronger version of working backwards.” The key, say experts, is to avoid rollout for its own sake. “We see mistakes happening due to poorly defined problems and a lack of understanding of the technology without the necessary subject matter expertise,” says Mr Garciga. “My fundamental advice is: be deliberate, start small, keep it scoped, and make sure you have a clear problem you're trying to solve.” Companies consider a vast array of ideas for AI applications, then carefully narrow them down to select the best use cases. “We have an intake process, by which I mean a centralised function that [considers GenAI implementation ideas from across the company, and] the number of ideas keeps increasing every day,” says Mr Ulrich at Mastercard. “Through this we see ideas increasing in two ways.
nodes.author: Economist Impact

nodes.label: First, as we find what drives value, the percentage of ideas that make it throug
nodes.text: First, as we find what drives value, the percentage of ideas that make it through the funnel increases. And second, even if the percentage of ideas that are actually commercialised remains small, you will still see a lot happening because we’re growing the denominator of ideas.”
nodes.author: Economist Impact

nodes.label: “Fewer than 20% of AI pilots end up in production. It requires a heap of experim
nodes.text: “Fewer than 20% of AI pilots end up in production. It requires a heap of experimentation in working out what’s going to create value.”
nodes.author: Economist Impact

nodes.label: Gereurd Roberts, group managing director, Seven Digital, Seven West Media Senthi
nodes.text: Gereurd Roberts, group managing director, Seven Digital, Seven West Media Senthil Ramani, the global lead for data and AI at Accenture, a global consultancy, says the firm sees a distinction emerging between use cases.
nodes.author: Economist Impact

nodes.label: Table stakes refer to more straightforward use cases, which include optimising c
nodes.text: Table stakes refer to more straightforward use cases, which include optimising customer contact centre operations, marketing functions or coding workflows.
nodes.author: Economist Impact

nodes.label: Strategic bets represent more innovative and experimental use cases.
nodes.text: Strategic bets represent more innovative and experimental use cases.
nodes.author: Economist Impact

nodes.label: Sandbox environments are one mechanism for AI experimentation; a space not only
nodes.text: Sandbox environments are one mechanism for AI experimentation; a space not only to test the technical aspects of an ML model for hallucination, bias and performance, but also its business impact and validity.
nodes.author: Economist Impact

nodes.label: Scott Hallworth, the chief data and analytics officer at HP, a technology compan
nodes.text: Scott Hallworth, the chief data and analytics officer at HP, a technology company, is a strong proponent.
nodes.author: Economist Impact

nodes.label: He embraced the sandbox approach early on in the GenAI boom, where all HP employ
nodes.text: He embraced the sandbox approach early on in the GenAI boom, where all HP employees had access to an environment where they could explore and test solutions that can make life easier for HP staff and customers.
nodes.author: Economist Impact

nodes.label: HP today supports 75 private sandboxes for a variety of uses all in the name of
nodes.text: HP today supports 75 private sandboxes for a variety of uses all in the name of testing and exploring the possibilities that AI can bring to the organisation in a safe, secure and robust environment.
nodes.author: Economist Impact

nodes.label: Key takeaways
nodes.text: Key takeaways
nodes.author: Economist Impact

nodes.label: Take a stake-based approach to experimentation. Cognisant of the risks and unkno
nodes.text: Take a stake-based approach to experimentation. Cognisant of the risks and unknowns,
nodes.author: Economist Impact

nodes.label: firms are focusing on internal use-cases in lower-stakes domains as they ascend
nodes.text: firms are focusing on internal use-cases in lower-stakes domains as they ascend the maturation curve, and should focus on small, tightly scoped, problem-oriented projects.
nodes.author: Economist Impact

nodes.label: Sandboxes and centralised oversight for pilots can help coordinate
nodes.text: Sandboxes and centralised oversight for pilots can help coordinate
nodes.author: Economist Impact

nodes.label: experiments. ‘Letting a hundred flowers bloom’ could impede long-term coherence
nodes.text: experiments. ‘Letting a hundred flowers bloom’ could impede long-term coherence and governance of AI projects. Sandboxes, testing environments and institutions to evaluate and greenlight experiments can help bring order.
nodes.author: Economist Impact

nodes.label: In a volatile world, AI can parse signal from noise. Firms reliant
nodes.text: In a volatile world, AI can parse signal from noise. Firms reliant
nodes.author: Economist Impact

nodes.label: on international supply chains and sensitive to economic and political headwinds
nodes.text: on international supply chains and sensitive to economic and political headwinds are leaning on AI to inform decision-making.
nodes.author: Economist Impact

nodes.label: As AI spending soars, businesses are grappling with the challenge of balancing e
nodes.text: As AI spending soars, businesses are grappling with the challenge of balancing enthusiasm with disciplined investment strategies and realistic expectations for returns.
nodes.author: Economist Impact

nodes.label: While internal operational improvements have been the initial focus, companies a
nodes.text: While internal operational improvements have been the initial focus, companies are now shifting towards external AI applications that could drive significant revenue growth and unlock value from previously untapped data sources.
nodes.author: Economist Impact

nodes.label: However, measuring AI success requires patience and a nuanced approach as organi
nodes.text: However, measuring AI success requires patience and a nuanced approach as organisations navigate the complex landscape of long-term value creation versus short-term gains in the AI-driven future.
nodes.author: Economist Impact

nodes.label: Earlier in 2024 a research note from Goldman Sachs cautioned that the US$1trn of
nodes.text: Earlier in 2024 a research note from Goldman Sachs cautioned that the US$1trn of expected spending on GenAI might risk running ahead of the revenue prospects.11 The bear case included GenAI being ill suited to solving the kinds of complex problems that would justify this spend and cost.
nodes.author: Economist Impact

nodes.label: The C-suite executives interviewed for this report regard the questioning of Gen
nodes.text: The C-suite executives interviewed for this report regard the questioning of GenAI’s value proposition as welcome, and part of a natural cycle; hype followed by short-term disillusion, succeeded by proof points of value over time.
nodes.author: Economist Impact

nodes.label: Our survey found that revenue growth has been the least important metric for AI
nodes.text: Our survey found that revenue growth has been the least important metric for AI investment to date, with only 19% of respondents agreeing that it has significantly contributed to the investment case (see figure 4).
nodes.author: Economist Impact

nodes.label: Executives are understandably focused on the long-term view and nervous about fa
nodes.text: Executives are understandably focused on the long-term view and nervous about falling prey to underinvestment.
nodes.author: Economist Impact

nodes.label: In an August analyst call, Alphabet CEO Sundar Pichai said that the risk of unde
nodes.text: In an August analyst call, Alphabet CEO Sundar Pichai said that the risk of underinvesting in AI was “dramatically greater” than of overinvesting.
nodes.author: Economist Impact

nodes.label: Microsoft, Alphabet, Amazon and Meta Platforms are expected to incur more than U
nodes.text: Microsoft, Alphabet, Amazon and Meta Platforms are expected to incur more than US$200bn in capital expenditure in 2024, most of which is to build AI infrastructure.12 But investors do expect AI to shift from a ‘tell me’ to a ‘show me’ story, with current disconnects between investments and revenue generation to come under increased scrutiny.13 This may be years away, not quarters, for many enterprise adopters.
nodes.author: Economist Impact

nodes.label: But vendors and firms with tangible GenAI offerings are already posting revenue
nodes.text: But vendors and firms with tangible GenAI offerings are already posting revenue gains, as are the ‘pick and shovel’ industries like cloud computing, chips and data centres.
nodes.author: Economist Impact

nodes.label: The cloud market, for instance, could reach US$2trn on the back of AI workflows,
nodes.text: The cloud market, for instance, could reach US$2trn on the back of AI workflows, according to one forecast.14 Meanwhile, our survey found that six in ten enterprise architects are increasing their reliance on cloud services for AI, with a shift towards multi-cloud environments for just over a third.
nodes.author: Economist Impact

nodes.label: Our survey also shows that deployment plans of companies will turn outwards over
nodes.text: Our survey also shows that deployment plans of companies will turn outwards over the next three years, shifting from internal purposes like operational improvement to external use cases (see figure 5).
nodes.author: Economist Impact

nodes.label: This may accelerate the revenue opportunity for AI as compared with using it for
nodes.text: This may accelerate the revenue opportunity for AI as compared with using it for optimising business as usual.
nodes.author: Economist Impact

nodes.label: Figure 4: Revenue growth is not yet a leading driver of AI investment decisions
nodes.text: Figure 4: Revenue growth is not yet a leading driver of AI investment decisions

nodes.label: Apple Unveils iPhone 16”, 2024, https://www.investors.com/news/technology/artifi
nodes.text: Apple Unveils iPhone 16”, 2024, https://www.investors.com/news/technology/artificial-intelligence-stocks/ 14 Goldman Sachs, “Cloud revenues poised to reach $2 trillion by 2030 amid AI rollout”, 2024, https://www.goldmansachs.com/insights/articles/cloud-revenues-poised-to-reach-2-trillion-by-2030-amid-ai-rollout Indeed, many respondents (37%) are starting to see more benefits from external rather than internal use cases.
nodes.author: Economist Impact

nodes.label: This is noteworthy, given that only 24% of organisations allocate the majority o
nodes.text: This is noteworthy, given that only 24% of organisations allocate the majority of their investment to external use cases (see figure 6).
nodes.author: Economist Impact

nodes.label: It suggests that some organisations—likely those on the vanguard of expanding an
nodes.text: It suggests that some organisations—likely those on the vanguard of expanding and scaling—are beginning to see outsized ROI from their external GenAI applications.
nodes.author: Economist Impact

nodes.label: As the remaining 88% reach this stage in the coming years, ROI may shift even mo
nodes.text: As the remaining 88% reach this stage in the coming years, ROI may shift even more strongly towards external use cases.
nodes.author: Economist Impact

nodes.label: Mr Ramani at Accenture highlights that productivity is a sensible rationale for
nodes.text: Mr Ramani at Accenture highlights that productivity is a sensible rationale for initial adoption and easier to measure, but the organisations set to become tomorrow’s AI leaders will be those that transcend productivity into understanding that AI is about the “growth of revenue, new opportunities, and attracting and retaining talent”.
nodes.author: Economist Impact

nodes.text: Figure 6: Investments versus benefits
