nodes.label: Adoption of GenAl models, by type Currently using in production (ie, fully deplo
nodes.text: Adoption of GenAl models, by type Currently using in production (ie, fully deployed) Experimenting/piloting Not using now but planning to use in the next three years Not using now and not planning to use in the next three years Bring your own Al: facilitate and govern employees' use of public models Use Al embedded in other software packages Build applications by using APIs to connect to proprietary models Train a custom model based on your own data or third-party data Licence or subscribe to a commercial version of a public model Serve or deploy an open-source model Fine-tune and engineer pre-trained open-source models 28 Economist Impact, “Open sourcing the AI revolution”, 2024, https://impact.economist.com/perspectives/technology-innovation/open-sourcing-ai-revolution Companies and organisations have access to the best fuel for AI models: the right and most relevant data, not merely data sourced from the open internet.
nodes.author: Economist Impact

nodes.label: Dream Sports, for instance, found third-party LLMs ineffective, even if they wer
nodes.text: Dream Sports, for instance, found third-party LLMs ineffective, even if they were using the latest application, because the company’s language is too specific. Models have to be trained on the company’s data.
nodes.author: Economist Impact

nodes.label: Higher quality internal data improves the efficiency and performance of models.
nodes.text: Higher quality internal data improves the efficiency and performance of models.
nodes.author: Economist Impact

nodes.label: For instance, Rakuten built a deep learning foundation and embeddings using far
nodes.text: For instance, Rakuten built a deep learning foundation and embeddings using far less processing power because it trains them based on transaction data from its e-commerce site, according to the company’s chief AI and data officer, Ting Cai.
nodes.author: Economist Impact

nodes.label: "We can encode more information in fewer dimensions because we have high-quality
nodes.text: "We can encode more information in fewer dimensions because we have high-quality transaction data that better indicates our users’ interests, and using this data allows us to create more efficient models.” Shell is also adapting foundational models to work on internal data, for example, customer interactions or past contracts. “I'm not too worried about hallucination in the language models Shell uses because of the controls we have in place before deployment.
nodes.author: Economist Impact

nodes.label: We restrict these models to work only with our controlled data, which has gone t
nodes.text: We restrict these models to work only with our controlled data, which has gone through the necessary rigour, including the right human supervision, before becoming a part of our work processes,” says Robbert Van Rutten, chief information officer of Shell, the energy company. “We are also working on developing our own models by pre-training or fine- tuning LLMs to further minimise this risk.” “We can encode more information in fewer dimensions because we have high-quality transaction data that better indicates our users’ interests, and using this data allows us to create more efficient models.” Ting Cai, chief AI and data officer, Rakuten Group Companies can, of course, use third-party models for initial tinkering before deciding where to invest more technical resources.
nodes.author: Economist Impact

nodes.label: At Repsol, for example, the company fed internal data into existing LLMs offered
nodes.text: At Repsol, for example, the company fed internal data into existing LLMs offered by companies like OpenAI, Microsoft and Google rather than building a GenAI pipeline from scratch as it experiments with use cases and seeks to explore the technology’s potential. “In the future, the market will move to the use of small private LLMs,” predicts Juan Jose Casado, chief digital officer at the Spanish energy company. “But GenAI is a new technology.
nodes.author: Economist Impact

nodes.label: We still need to learn how to launch and put initiatives into production in an a
nodes.text: We still need to learn how to launch and put initiatives into production in an agile way.” Home-grown models are also appealing because they put companies in control of the data.
nodes.author: Economist Impact

nodes.label: Many are worried about data leakage in closed-source LLMs, such as sensitive or
nodes.text: Many are worried about data leakage in closed-source LLMs, such as sensitive or restricted material being dropped into external models. “We have strong views on owning and protecting our data, so we’re very cautious about where we let our data go,” says Ms Clements at JetBlue. “We don’t let it leave JetBlue.”
nodes.author: Economist Impact

nodes.label: To unlock value and find their edge, companies will mix and match off-the-shelf
nodes.text: To unlock value and find their edge, companies will mix and match off-the-shelf AI with
nodes.author: Economist Impact

nodes.label: open-source and in-house models. AI will soon be a baseline capability; the orga
nodes.text: open-source and in-house models. AI will soon be a baseline capability; the organisations that excel will mix and match models to complement their unique data and know-how. Only they have access to the best fuel for AI models: the right and most relevant data.
nodes.author: Economist Impact

nodes.label: AI promises to unlock proprietary data that was hitherto hidden or dormant. This
nodes.text: AI promises to unlock proprietary data that was hitherto hidden or dormant. This
nodes.author: Economist Impact

nodes.label: will drive higher-performing models through accuracy, efficiency and relevance.
nodes.text: will drive higher-performing models through accuracy, efficiency and relevance.
nodes.author: Economist Impact

nodes.label: Off-the- shelf AI will be useful for low-cost tinkering and lower-value outputs.
nodes.text: Off-the- shelf AI will be useful for low-cost tinkering and lower-value outputs.
nodes.author: Economist Impact

nodes.label: For true value creation, and for greater control and security, companies will co
nodes.text: For true value creation, and for greater control and security, companies will combine open- and closed-source models, while larger firms may see value in developing their own models from scratch.
nodes.author: Economist Impact

nodes.label: “In the future, the market will move to the use of small private LLMs. But GenAI
nodes.text: “In the future, the market will move to the use of small private LLMs. But GenAI is a new technology. We still need to learn how to launch and put initiatives into production in an agile way.”
nodes.author: Economist Impact

nodes.label: Juan Jose Casado, chief digital officer, Repsol As AI becomes ubiquitous, organi
nodes.text: Juan Jose Casado, chief digital officer, Repsol As AI becomes ubiquitous, organisations face a complex web of risks, from data breaches to regulatory compliance.
nodes.author: Economist Impact

nodes.label: Companies are developing multifaceted governance approaches, from institutional
nodes.text: Companies are developing multifaceted governance approaches, from institutional measures such as AI centres of excellence, principle- based approaches like human-in-the-loop guardrails, and deployment of innovative technical solutions like synthetic data and RAG to improve AI reliability.
nodes.author: Economist Impact

nodes.label: The challenge lies in striking the delicate balance between harnessing AI's tran
nodes.text: The challenge lies in striking the delicate balance between harnessing AI's transformative power and maintaining robust safeguards, all while adapting to an ever-evolving regulatory environment and calibrating human judgement and machine intelligence.
nodes.author: Economist Impact

nodes.label: Data security and governance had already become board-level priorities over the
nodes.text: Data security and governance had already become board-level priorities over the last decade as cybersecurity attacks—and the regulatory punishments—have grown more severe.
nodes.author: Economist Impact

nodes.label: A flurry of regulations like the Digital Operational Resilience Act in Europe ar
nodes.text: A flurry of regulations like the Digital Operational Resilience Act in Europe are also raising the stakes of governance failures.
nodes.author: Economist Impact

nodes.label: Our survey underscores these priorities, finding that data privacy and security
nodes.text: Our survey underscores these priorities, finding that data privacy and security breaches are the top concern for 53% of enterprise architects, while security and governance are the most challenging aspects of data engineering for engineers (cited by 50% as a top-three challenge).
nodes.author: Economist Impact

nodes.label: “Numerous issues, such as privacy protection, security measures, IP issues, ethi
nodes.text: “Numerous issues, such as privacy protection, security measures, IP issues, ethical concerns and the problem of deepfakes need to be addressed,” says Mr Sato of NTT Docomo. “It is essential to maximise the benefits of AI technology by overcoming these challenges in collaboration with relevant parties,” he adds.
nodes.author: Economist Impact

nodes.label: Worldwide, legislation is ratcheting up. One 2024 tracker estimated a total of 7
nodes.text: Worldwide, legislation is ratcheting up. One 2024 tracker estimated a total of 762 AI legislation documents across 45 states in the US alone.29 This poses a challenge for companies that need to experiment with new systems while the rules governing their use are in constant flux.
nodes.author: Economist Impact

nodes.label: According to Ian Botts, chief technology officer of Fanatics Betting & Gaming, e
nodes.text: According to Ian Botts, chief technology officer of Fanatics Betting & Gaming, effective AI governance at an organisation encompasses three components. “First, it's building the guardrails for what you can and can't do, so that personally identifiable information isn't used inside of a model to specifically target or take advantage of a particular human.
nodes.author: Economist Impact

nodes.label: Second, it's mechanisms—and
nodes.text: Second, it's mechanisms—and
nodes.author: Economist Impact

nodes.label: Data privacy and security breaches are the top concern for 53% of enterprise arc
nodes.text: Data privacy and security breaches are the top concern for 53% of enterprise architects, while security and governance are the most challenging aspects of data engineering for engineers.
nodes.author: Economist Impact

nodes.label: 29 Regulatory Transparency Project, “A Sensible Approach to State AI Policy”, ht
nodes.text: 29 Regulatory Transparency Project, “A Sensible Approach to State AI Policy”, https://rtp.fedsoc.org/blog/a-sensible-approach- to-state-ai-policy/?utm_content=311457390&utm_medium=social&utm_source=twitter&hss_channel=tw-574405888 building out mechanisms—that enforce those guardrails.
nodes.author: Economist Impact

nodes.label: And third, it's having dedicated data governance, which is almost like a complia
nodes.text: And third, it's having dedicated data governance, which is almost like a compliance function to ensure that it's being audited and that [companies are] being transparent.
nodes.author: Economist Impact

nodes.label: I think the transparency point is incredibly important because it drives better
nodes.text: I think the transparency point is incredibly important because it drives better decisions.” Data innovations can help companies tackle governance risks like bias or unreliability.
nodes.author: Economist Impact

nodes.label: For example, some organisations may lack access to the necessary data to build w
nodes.text: For example, some organisations may lack access to the necessary data to build what they need, in turn limiting the quality of their AI systems.
nodes.author: Economist Impact

nodes.label: Synthetic data, generated using a purpose-built mathematical model or algorithm,
nodes.text: Synthetic data, generated using a purpose-built mathematical model or algorithm, can be brought in to help solve these data science tasks.30 It can improve performance and reliability by providing a larger volume of more diverse data for training, such as including underrepresented data classes or categories, for instance.
nodes.author: Economist Impact

nodes.label: Closing gender gaps and eliminating racial biases are promising areas where synt
nodes.text: Closing gender gaps and eliminating racial biases are promising areas where synthetic data can make a difference.31 It can also lower data acquisition costs, eliminate data bottlenecks and preserve data privacy.
nodes.author: Economist Impact

nodes.label: While it has risks and downsides, with the right guardrails, synthetic data coul
nodes.text: While it has risks and downsides, with the right guardrails, synthetic data could help improve the quality of AI outputs, but it remains an untapped resource, according to our survey, with only 7.5% of data scientists utilising it in their GenAI projects.
nodes.author: Economist Impact

nodes.label: RAG is another useful technique to control quality and enrich knowledge. “With t
nodes.text: RAG is another useful technique to control quality and enrich knowledge. “With this technique, Rakuten’s GenAI models retrieve real-time inventory and pricing information that they can use to incorporate the latest information or enterprise knowledge into applications such as search, recommendation systems, and adverts,” says Mr Cai, the company’s chief AI and data officer.
nodes.author: Economist Impact

nodes.label: Ian Botts at Fanatics also explains how its AI and GenAI models are being used t
nodes.text: Ian Botts at Fanatics also explains how its AI and GenAI models are being used to detect and enhance governance issues related to responsible gaming. “We want your bet to be additive to your fandom,” he says. “If [our models] identify someone and say with very high accuracy that there's a problem that spend is exceeding their means, or they're showing troubling behaviours or patterns, [we] could start to nudge them into free-to-play games, lower cost things, try to reduce that burn or encourage them to take a timeout.
nodes.author: Economist Impact

nodes.label: Present them with information.
nodes.text: Present them with information.
nodes.author: Economist Impact

nodes.label: It’s worth the additional investment … to get someone back into a position that’
nodes.text: It’s worth the additional investment … to get someone back into a position that’s healthy and sustainable for them.
nodes.author: Economist Impact

nodes.label: AI can be used to spot and understand problems, and GenAI can be used to underst
nodes.text: AI can be used to spot and understand problems, and GenAI can be used to understand and influence [in a personalised way].” 30 The Alan Turing Institute and The Royal Society, “Synthetic data - what, why and how?”, https:// royalsociety.org/-/media/policy/projects/privacy- enhancing-technologies/Synthetic_Data_Survey-24.pdf 31 UNU Macau, “Bridging the Gender Data Gap: Harnessing Synthetic Data for Inclusive AI”, 2024, https://unu.edu/ macau/blog-post/bridging-gender-data-gap-harnessing- synthetic-data-inclusive-ai
nodes.author: Economist Impact

nodes.label: Figure 13: Four in ten say their organisation’s AI governance is insufficient
nodes.text: Figure 13: Four in ten say their organisation’s AI governance is insufficient
nodes.author: Economist Impact

nodes.label: My organisation has implemented sufficient processes to ensure Al safety and com
nodes.text: My organisation has implemented sufficient processes to ensure Al safety and compliance. [Agree or disagree] Somewhat disagree Strongly disagree Don't know / not applicable Somewhat agree Neutral Strongly agree While technical innovations are part of the governance toolkit, companies also need to consider forming new internal structures and institutions to co-ordinate AI experiments and embed best practices.
nodes.author: Economist Impact

nodes.label: Many of the large multinational enterprise executives interviewed for this repor
nodes.text: Many of the large multinational enterprise executives interviewed for this report highlighted the importance of unified systems, clear processes and strong governance.
nodes.author: Economist Impact

nodes.label: Our survey found lagging progress, however: 40% of respondents acknowledged that
nodes.text: Our survey found lagging progress, however: 40% of respondents acknowledged that their organisation’s processes to ensure AI safety and compliance are insufficient (see figure 13).
nodes.author: Economist Impact

nodes.label: Companies of all sizes report similar perceptions about the quality of their gov
nodes.text: Companies of all sizes report similar perceptions about the quality of their governance.
nodes.author: Economist Impact

nodes.label: However, differences across geographies were more pronounced, with respondents i
nodes.text: However, differences across geographies were more pronounced, with respondents in India (35%) and the US (29%) most likely to strongly agree that their processes were sufficient compared with the global average (21%).
nodes.author: Economist Impact

nodes.label: “With GenAI, there is an opportunity to revisit an entire business process and r
nodes.text: “With GenAI, there is an opportunity to revisit an entire business process and reimagine what it could be.
nodes.author: Economist Impact

nodes.label: It encourages leaders to challenge past decisions and accept that there may be a
nodes.text: It encourages leaders to challenge past decisions and accept that there may be a newer, better way.” Ryan Snyder, senior vice president and chief information officer, Thermo Fisher Scientific Centres of excellence (COEs) are emerging as a popular approach to achieving systematic oversight.
nodes.author: Economist Impact

nodes.label: COEs have become key enablers of AI- driven insights and use cases within organi
nodes.text: COEs have become key enablers of AI- driven insights and use cases within organisations.
nodes.author: Economist Impact

nodes.label: Beyond acting as the central control unit of AI, they monitor, implement and sha
nodes.text: Beyond acting as the central control unit of AI, they monitor, implement and share best practices for implementation across departments and business units.
nodes.author: Economist Impact

nodes.label: They also foster exploration and innovation for building and operationalising AI
nodes.text: They also foster exploration and innovation for building and operationalising AI.32 Accenture’s Mr Ramani relates that his company collaborated with one national bank to build a COE to manage guidelines and protocols on how AI is used and adopted across the bank’s business units.
nodes.author: Economist Impact

nodes.label: While centres can forge consistency in practices and create guidelines and guard
nodes.text: While centres can forge consistency in practices and create guidelines and guardrails, they are not just brakes on AI.
nodes.author: Economist Impact

nodes.label: They can actually be a kind of ‘forcing function’ for AI delivery across the ent
nodes.text: They can actually be a kind of ‘forcing function’ for AI delivery across the enterprise, according to Mr Ramani, bringing together departments, from HR and legal to tech teams, to unlock potential.
nodes.author: Economist Impact

nodes.label: A COE is a collaborative effort bringing together multidisciplinary teams and ba
nodes.text: A COE is a collaborative effort bringing together multidisciplinary teams and backgrounds for a common goal.
nodes.author: Economist Impact

nodes.label: For example, Shell’s Analytics COE brings together multi-faceted teams and suppo
nodes.text: For example, Shell’s Analytics COE brings together multi-faceted teams and supports a community of hundreds of data scientists and thousands of what the company calls ‘AI enthusiasts’ coming together.
nodes.author: Economist Impact

nodes.label: Technically, COEs develop, ‘productionise’ and monitor AI models with a lens on
nodes.text: Technically, COEs develop, ‘productionise’ and monitor AI models with a lens on business impact and returns.
nodes.author: Economist Impact

nodes.label: They oversee core operations, including for data and ML, and seek architecture a
nodes.text: They oversee core operations, including for data and ML, and seek architecture and infrastructure optimisations for operational efficiencies.
nodes.author: Economist Impact

nodes.label: Most importantly, they unify the organisation’s AI vision and ambitions across b
nodes.text: Most importantly, they unify the organisation’s AI vision and ambitions across business units and channels, while ensuring that safety and standards are met.
nodes.author: Economist Impact

nodes.label: Measures like COEs are examples of how companies can take the opportunity of the
nodes.text: Measures like COEs are examples of how companies can take the opportunity of the AI moment to revisit all aspects of governance and decision-making. “With GenAI, there is an opportunity to revisit an entire business process and reimagine what it could be,” says Mr Snyder at Thermo Fisher Scientific. “It encourages leaders to challenge past decisions and accept that there may be a newer, better way.” 32 Deloitte, “Is your AI center of excellence still a center of experimentation”, https://www2.deloitte.com/content/dam/Deloitte/us/Documents/consulting/us-is-your-ai-center-of-excellence-still- center-of-experimentation.pdf
nodes.author: Economist Impact

nodes.label: Only one in six firms are confident in their ability to attract the expertise ne
nodes.text: Only one in six firms are confident in their ability to attract the expertise needed to take full advantage of AI technologies. Those who succeed are deploying AI at a much faster rate.
nodes.author: Economist Impact

nodes.label: The march of the machines has long stirred fears of widespread job losses as aut
nodes.text: The march of the machines has long stirred fears of widespread job losses as automation threatens to render human workers obsolete.
nodes.author: Economist Impact

nodes.label: Yet, as AI technologies permeate industries, the reality is more nuanced.
nodes.text: Yet, as AI technologies permeate industries, the reality is more nuanced.
nodes.author: Economist Impact

nodes.label: Many business leaders emphasise that AI adoption will not lead to layoffs. “AI m
nodes.text: Many business leaders emphasise that AI adoption will not lead to layoffs. “AI means more efficiency in our business, which means we can create more stories—not by using AI to generate content but by automating mundane or manual tasks,” says Mr Bhakta at Condé Nast. “So instead of ten a week, we could produce 30 a week, which is actually good for the business.
nodes.author: Economist Impact

nodes.label: We're not looking to shrink, we're looking to grow.
nodes.text: We're not looking to shrink, we're looking to grow.
nodes.author: Economist Impact

nodes.label: Doing more with the number of people we have is the best outcome.” As mass AI ad
nodes.text: Doing more with the number of people we have is the best outcome.” As mass AI adoption takes hold, there will be more demand for specialised roles like data scientists, developers and MLOps experts.
nodes.author: Economist Impact

nodes.label: Companies are already paying premiums for workers with AI-related abilities, and
nodes.text: Companies are already paying premiums for workers with AI-related abilities, and those who acquire AI skills see wages rise by an average of 21%.33,34 One 2030 forecast estimates that 375 million workers may need to switch occupations as their line of work is disrupted by digitalisation, automation and AI.35 Securing talent is one of the greatest challenges for firms deploying AI, our survey found; only one in six firms are confident in their ability to attract the expertise needed to take full advantage of AI technologies.
nodes.author: Economist Impact

nodes.label: Those who succeed are deploying AI at a much faster rate.
nodes.text: Those who succeed are deploying AI at a much faster rate.
nodes.author: Economist Impact

nodes.label: Meanwhile, the proliferation of GenAI is also seen as a democratising force.
nodes.text: Meanwhile, the proliferation of GenAI is also seen as a democratising force.
nodes.author: Economist Impact

nodes.label: More than 97% of enterprise architects in our survey predict that non-technical
nodes.text: More than 97% of enterprise architects in our survey predict that non-technical staff will use natural language programmes to interact with complex datasets within the next three years, and 58% say that natural language will be the primary—or only—way they do this.
nodes.author: Economist Impact

nodes.label: Unilever, for instance, is investing in both people and culture through a progra
nodes.text: Unilever, for instance, is investing in both people and culture through a programme that has trained almost 20,000 people in the use of GenAI, a vital step for a company operating in a dynamic sector where demand for new skills and competencies is rising, according to Mr Hill.
nodes.author: Economist Impact

nodes.label: “A data and AI culture helps all parts of the business understand that we priori
nodes.text: “A data and AI culture helps all parts of the business understand that we prioritise data- driven decisions, and that’s what will help us gain the insights that will improve performance,” says Mr Roberts at Seven West Media. “[Our] priority is to train and upskill our teams and talent around GenAI, so that it becomes a process and a product that's internalised.” 33 Foote Partners LLC, “2024 IT Skills & Certifications Pay Index”, https://footepartners.com/collections/pay-index 34 Fabian Stephany, Ole Teutloff, "What is the price of a skill?
nodes.author: Economist Impact

nodes.label: The value of complementarity", Research Policy, Volume 53, Issue 1, 2024, https:
nodes.text: The value of complementarity", Research Policy, Volume 53, Issue 1, 2024, https://doi.org/10.1016/j. respol.2023.104898. 35 McKinsey, “Retraining and reskilling workers in the age of automation”, 2018, https://www.mckinsey.com/featured- insights/future-of-work/retraining-and-reskilling- workers-in-the-age-of-automation Firms like Condé Nast and Frontier are prioritising engagement with employees, emphasising the advantages of automation rather than presenting it as a threat.
nodes.author: Economist Impact

nodes.label: Their approach highlights how AI can ease workloads and enhance job performance
nodes.text: Their approach highlights how AI can ease workloads and enhance job performance rather than eliminate positions.
nodes.author: Economist Impact

nodes.label: Some firms are embedding AI tools into office software to ease workloads. “Our N
nodes.text: Some firms are embedding AI tools into office software to ease workloads. “Our North Star is to democratise data.
nodes.author: Economist Impact

nodes.label: Everyone at the company should be able to leverage data that drives decisions …
nodes.text: Everyone at the company should be able to leverage data that drives decisions … without being a data engineer or a data scientist,” says Mr Bensaid at Rivian. “Traditional AI involved specialised teams in expert environments,” says Mr Bucher at Novartis. “GenAI is accessible to many users, with self- explanatory tools and understandable use cases.” Once a new technology has been developed and proven to be successful, the crucial step for a company is to develop self-service tools so employees can benefit, says Mr Casado at Repsol. “Technicians will deal with the technology—the real challenge is making it easy for the people to use [it],” he says.
nodes.author: Economist Impact

nodes.label: The Repsol Artificial Intelligence Products hands employees the keys to AI, lett
nodes.text: The Repsol Artificial Intelligence Products hands employees the keys to AI, letting them tackle complex data tasks solo, without technical knowledge.36 Staff can analyse performance and optimise operations and organisational change with natural language processors.
nodes.author: Economist Impact

nodes.label: These tools promise autonomy and agility, allowing Repsol to streamline operatio
nodes.text: These tools promise autonomy and agility, allowing Repsol to streamline operations while turning its workforce into “citizen data scientists”.
nodes.author: Economist Impact

nodes.label: Self-service platforms can empower workers to handle tasks once left to speciali
nodes.text: Self-service platforms can empower workers to handle tasks once left to specialists, like processing payroll, scheduling meetings or crunching numbers.
nodes.author: Economist Impact

nodes.label: The appeal for employers is more efficiency, fewer middlemen and lower costs.
nodes.text: The appeal for employers is more efficiency, fewer middlemen and lower costs.
nodes.author: Economist Impact

nodes.label: However, simply making AI available is not enough.
nodes.text: However, simply making AI available is not enough.
nodes.author: Economist Impact

nodes.label: Broader use of AI requires broader data literacy.
nodes.text: Broader use of AI requires broader data literacy.
nodes.author: Economist Impact

nodes.label: The biggest data management challenges identified by architects in our survey, f
nodes.text: The biggest data management challenges identified by architects in our survey, for instance, include controlling data access (49%) and protecting sensitive data (42%).
nodes.author: Economist Impact

nodes.text: “Our North Star is to democratise data.

nodes.text: Everyone at the company should be able to leverage data that drives decisions … without being a data engineer or a data scientist.”

nodes.text: Wassym Bensaid, chief software officer, Rivian 36 Repsol, “The RAIP Project develops Repsol's own AI solutions with company-wide impact and value”, https://www.repsol.com/en/technology-and-digitalization/digital-transformation/digital-program/raip/index.cshtml “We’re moving away from data literacy being a specialised skill,” says Mr Garciga at the US Army.
nodes.author: Economist Impact

nodes.label: All employees must understand data protection, platform use and centralised data
nodes.text: All employees must understand data protection, platform use and centralised data storage.
nodes.author: Economist Impact

nodes.label: The US Army has incorporated these into its leadership training and has recognis
nodes.text: The US Army has incorporated these into its leadership training and has recognised that managing sensitive data is crucial to the Army’s AI use cases.
nodes.author: Economist Impact

nodes.label: One such use case involves reviewing documents to be declassified under the Free
nodes.text: One such use case involves reviewing documents to be declassified under the Freedom of Information Act, which allows for important documents related to events like the war in Afghanistan to be released to the public. “AI can improve the accuracy of this process and help us cover more documents, increasing transparency,” says Mr Garciga.
nodes.author: Economist Impact

nodes.label: Human judgement will remain critical for monitoring AI’s outputs, and companies
nodes.text: Human judgement will remain critical for monitoring AI’s outputs, and companies must calibrate the right mix of people and machines.
nodes.author: Economist Impact

nodes.label: Two-thirds of organisations in our survey say they are actively experimenting to
nodes.text: Two-thirds of organisations in our survey say they are actively experimenting to find the balance between humans and AI, and 80% say these efforts could be stronger.
nodes.author: Economist Impact

nodes.label: Mr Sato at NTT Docomo emphasises that “individuals using GenAI must have the abi
nodes.text: Mr Sato at NTT Docomo emphasises that “individuals using GenAI must have the ability to independently evaluate its outputs and handle them responsibly.” Progress is most advanced in the financial services industry, where AI must fall within strict regulatory guidelines, our survey found. “As organisations begin using AI to inform key decisions, they must still work within agreed upon risk models.
nodes.author: Economist Impact

nodes.label: For example, any approach that TD takes to leverage AI must be within our risk a
nodes.text: For example, any approach that TD takes to leverage AI must be within our risk appetite and regulatory obligations,” says Mr Martin at TD.
nodes.author: Economist Impact

nodes.label: That holds true for customer-facing interactions too. “In the old world, trust w
nodes.text: That holds true for customer-facing interactions too. “In the old world, trust was established by walking in the branch, getting to know the manager and talking about the product,” says Mr Kapoor at Mahindra Group. “Now trust has to be established through cybersecurity and data privacy, and the customer needs to know that data will not be misused for any purpose.” In sensitive sectors with high-risk profiles and low risk appetites, computer-generated errors will often provoke strong negative perceptions.
nodes.author: Economist Impact

nodes.label: As noted by Flo Health’s Mr Bugaev, medical specialists can also make mistakes o
nodes.text: As noted by Flo Health’s Mr Bugaev, medical specialists can also make mistakes or come to different answers to the same question, much like AI models might. But tolerance for errors and disparities in AI is understandably lower compared with tolerance for the same in medical professionals.
nodes.author: Economist Impact

nodes.text: Companies must calibrate the right mix of human and machine intelligence, especially in highly regulated industries where trust and risk management are paramount.

nodes.text: To build a robust data and technical foundation, companies need to invest in an infrastructure refit to support high-volume, varied data.

nodes.label: This will stress the rigid data storage systems of yesteryear, which cannot mana
nodes.text: This will stress the rigid data storage systems of yesteryear, which cannot manage the diversity of data, real-time usage and wider utilisation in this more democratised era.
nodes.author: Economist Impact

nodes.label: Larger companies and conglomerates especially must decide the right balance betw
nodes.text: Larger companies and conglomerates especially must decide the right balance between centralisation and devolution as they look to harness cloud platforms.

nodes.text: To avoid profligate spending and the inevitable trough of disillusionment that could follow, companies need to develop intelligent ROI metrics and a disciplined approach to spending.

nodes.label: Productivity, cost control, revenue per worker, user experience and reduced staf
nodes.text: Productivity, cost control, revenue per worker, user experience and reduced staff burnout are all meaningful metrics.

nodes.label: The most successful will use AI to drive new revenue streams and business model
nodes.text: The most successful will use AI to drive new revenue streams and business model innovation.

nodes.text: KPIs and metrics matter, but companies should not necessarily seek quick returns.

nodes.text: Experiments involve wrong turns and tinkering.

nodes.text: Companies in heavily regulated sectors like healthcare have to operate within structured systems of regulatory oversight.

nodes.label: It takes time to develop the high-quality data sets and governance needed to pow
nodes.text: It takes time to develop the high-quality data sets and governance needed to power effective AI.

nodes.label: There is also merit in ‘watchful waiting’ to see how the AI product and service
nodes.text: There is also merit in ‘watchful waiting’ to see how the AI product and service ecosystem evolves.

nodes.text: Use a mix of models to sharpen your edge: in-house data and organisational

nodes.text: All organisations need to find their edge—their unique recipe of data and know-how.

nodes.text: In highly specialised industries, off-the-shelf models will fall short of quality and compliance requirements.

nodes.label: Companies can mix and match open- and closed-source AI, and private or public cl
nodes.text: Companies can mix and match open- and closed-source AI, and private or public cloud, based on performance, governance, cost and capacity, to find the right blend.

nodes.text: Organisations need clear guidelines, protocols and oversight mechanisms, and institutional innovations like Centres of Excellence (COEs), to manage AI deployment and mitigate potential risks.

nodes.text: with employees in the design and deployment of AI systems is critical to secure buy-in and adoption.

nodes.text: Identifying AI champions can also help build momentum.

nodes.text: New skills and competencies can be developed through bite-sized programmes and exercises, with companies bridging the gap between AI specialists and the workforce.

nodes.text: Last, but not least, organisations should encourage a critical outlook to avoid automation bias and keep human judgement at the fore.

nodes.label: From AI Pilots to Production Reality: Architecture Lessons f
nodes.author: C4: Container, Code, Cloud & Context

nodes.label: A Beginning-of-Year Reflection for Enterprise Architects and Technical Leaders A
nodes.text: A Beginning-of-Year Reflection for Enterprise Architects and Technical Leaders As we step into 2026, it’s worth pausing to reflect on the seismic shifts that defined enterprise architecture in 2025—and the hard lessons learned when AI hype met production reality.
nodes.author: C4: Container, Code, Cloud & Context

nodes.label: What began as breathless excitement around generative AI and LLMs has matured in
nodes.text: What began as breathless excitement around generative AI and LLMs has matured into a more nuanced understanding: building AI proofs-of-concept is easy; operationalizing them at enterprise scale is where most initiatives still fail .
nodes.author: C4: Container, Code, Cloud & Context

nodes.label: This article reflects on the key architectural lessons from 2025 and outlines wh
nodes.text: This article reflects on the key architectural lessons from 2025 and outlines what 2026 demands from those of us responsible for building resilient, scalable, and cost-effective enterprise systems.
nodes.author: C4: Container, Code, Cloud & Context

nodes.label: The AI Journey from 2025 to 2026: From Pilot Failures to Production Demands
nodes.text: The AI Journey from 2025 to 2026: From Pilot Failures to Production Demands
nodes.author: C4: Container, Code, Cloud & Context

nodes.label: The Reality Check: Only 30% of AI Pilots Reach Production
nodes.text: The Reality Check: Only 30% of AI Pilots Reach Production
nodes.author: C4: Container, Code, Cloud & Context

nodes.label: CRITICAL FINDING: The 70% Failure Rate In 2025, organizations discovered an unco
nodes.text: CRITICAL FINDING: The 70% Failure Rate In 2025, organizations discovered an uncomfortable truth: approximately 7 out of 10 GenAI projects never made it past the pilot stage . The initial euphoria of ChatGPT-like demos quickly gave way to the brutal realities of enterprise deployment:
nodes.author: C4: Container, Code, Cloud & Context

nodes.label: Hallucination in production – LLMs confidently generating incorrect information
nodes.text: Hallucination in production – LLMs confidently generating incorrect information in customer-facing systems
nodes.author: C4: Container, Code, Cloud & Context

nodes.label: Cost explosion – What seemed affordable in dev/test became prohibitive at scale
nodes.text: Cost explosion – What seemed affordable in dev/test became prohibitive at scale
nodes.author: C4: Container, Code, Cloud & Context

nodes.label: Data governance nightmares – Legacy systems weren’t designed for the data access
nodes.text: Data governance nightmares – Legacy systems weren’t designed for the data accessibility AI demands
nodes.author: C4: Container, Code, Cloud & Context

nodes.label: Compliance failures – Healthcare (HIPAA), financial services (PCI-DSS), and EU (
nodes.text: Compliance failures – Healthcare (HIPAA), financial services (PCI-DSS), and EU (GDPR) requirements proving incompatible with early AI architectures
nodes.author: C4: Container, Code, Cloud & Context

nodes.label: Integration complexity – Bridging the gap between shiny new AI services and deca
nodes.text: Integration complexity – Bridging the gap between shiny new AI services and decades-old enterprise systems
nodes.author: C4: Container, Code, Cloud & Context

nodes.label: The architects who succeeded in 2025 weren’t necessarily the most innovative—the
nodes.text: The architects who succeeded in 2025 weren’t necessarily the most innovative—they were the most pragmatic. They focused on measurable ROI, robust governance, and modular architectures that could adapt as AI technology evolved at breakneck speed.
nodes.author: C4: Container, Code, Cloud & Context

nodes.label: Five Critical Architecture Lessons from 2025
nodes.text: Five Critical Architecture Lessons from 2025
nodes.author: C4: Container, Code, Cloud & Context

nodes.label: 1. Bigger Models ≠ Better Solutions
nodes.text: 1. Bigger Models ≠ Better Solutions
nodes.author: C4: Container, Code, Cloud & Context

nodes.label: The industry’s obsession with larger and larger language models hit a wall in 20
nodes.text: The industry’s obsession with larger and larger language models hit a wall in 2025. Organizations learned that model size isn’t always correlated with business value. What matters is the right model for the right task .
nodes.author: C4: Container, Code, Cloud & Context

nodes.label: We observed three key shifts:
nodes.text: We observed three key shifts:
nodes.author: C4: Container, Code, Cloud & Context

nodes.label: Specialized small language models (SLMs) fine-tuned for specific domains often o
nodes.text: Specialized small language models (SLMs) fine-tuned for specific domains often outperformed general-purpose LLMs on targeted tasks
nodes.author: C4: Container, Code, Cloud & Context

nodes.label: Domain-native AI with embedded regulatory compliance and industry context delive
nodes.text: Domain-native AI with embedded regulatory compliance and industry context delivered more reliable, trustworthy results
nodes.author: C4: Container, Code, Cloud & Context

nodes.label: Cost efficiency dramatically improved with right-sized models—some organizations
nodes.text: Cost efficiency dramatically improved with right-sized models—some organizations reduced inference costs by 60-80% through strategic model selection
nodes.author: C4: Container, Code, Cloud & Context

nodes.label: AI Model Selection Strategy 2026: Optimizing for Task Complexity and Response Ti
nodes.text: AI Model Selection Strategy 2026: Optimizing for Task Complexity and Response Time
nodes.author: C4: Container, Code, Cloud & Context

nodes.label: ARCHITECTURE PRINCIPLE: Model Diversity Over Monopoly Design your AI architectur
nodes.text: ARCHITECTURE PRINCIPLE: Model Diversity Over Monopoly Design your AI architecture to make model selection and swapping trivial.
nodes.author: C4: Container, Code, Cloud & Context

nodes.label: Use abstraction layers, standardized interfaces, and avoid tight coupling to spe
nodes.text: Use abstraction layers, standardized interfaces, and avoid tight coupling to specific model providers.
nodes.author: C4: Container, Code, Cloud & Context

nodes.label: Your platform should treat models as interchangeable components optimized for sp
nodes.text: Your platform should treat models as interchangeable components optimized for specific workloads.
nodes.author: C4: Container, Code, Cloud & Context

nodes.label: 2. RAG is Table Stakes, Not Innovation
nodes.text: 2. RAG is Table Stakes, Not Innovation
nodes.author: C4: Container, Code, Cloud & Context

nodes.label: Retrieval-Augmented Generation (RAG) transitioned from cutting-edge technique to
nodes.text: Retrieval-Augmented Generation (RAG) transitioned from cutting-edge technique to baseline expectation. By Q4 2025, any production LLM system without RAG was considered architecturally incomplete—like deploying a web application without HTTPS.
nodes.author: C4: Container, Code, Cloud & Context

nodes.label: The evolution of RAG matured through three distinct phases:
nodes.text: The evolution of RAG matured through three distinct phases:
nodes.author: C4: Container, Code, Cloud & Context

nodes.label: Evolution of RAG: From Basic to Agentic (2023-2026)
nodes.text: Evolution of RAG: From Basic to Agentic (2023-2026)
nodes.author: C4: Container, Code, Cloud & Context

nodes.label: Basic RAG (2023-2024) – Simple vector search + context injection. Effective but
nodes.text: Basic RAG (2023-2024) – Simple vector search + context injection. Effective but limited by single retrieval strategy.
nodes.author: C4: Container, Code, Cloud & Context

nodes.label: Hybrid RAG (2025) – Combining vector search, keyword matching, and knowledge gra
nodes.text: Hybrid RAG (2025) – Combining vector search, keyword matching, and knowledge graph traversal. Dramatically improved accuracy for complex queries.
nodes.author: C4: Container, Code, Cloud & Context

nodes.label: Agentic RAG (2026) – Autonomous agents that dynamically orchestrate multiple ret
nodes.text: Agentic RAG (2026) – Autonomous agents that dynamically orchestrate multiple retrieval strategies, validate sources, and adapt based on query characteristics and user context.
nodes.author: C4: Container, Code, Cloud & Context

nodes.label: ARCHITECTURE IMPLICATION: Data Architecture First Plan your data architecture to
nodes.text: ARCHITECTURE IMPLICATION: Data Architecture First Plan your data architecture to support evolving RAG patterns from day one.
nodes.author: C4: Container, Code, Cloud & Context

nodes.label: Invest in vector databases (Pinecone, Weaviate, Qdrant), semantic layers, and au
nodes.text: Invest in vector databases (Pinecone, Weaviate, Qdrant), semantic layers, and automated data quality pipelines.
nodes.author: C4: Container, Code, Cloud & Context

nodes.label: These aren’t nice-to-haves—they’re the foundation of trustworthy, hallucination-
nodes.text: These aren’t nice-to-haves—they’re the foundation of trustworthy, hallucination-resistant AI.
nodes.author: C4: Container, Code, Cloud & Context

nodes.label: Key investments: Vector indexing infrastructure, embedding generation pipelines,
nodes.text: Key investments: Vector indexing infrastructure, embedding generation pipelines, metadata enrichment, data versioning, and continuous quality monitoring.
nodes.author: C4: Container, Code, Cloud & Context

nodes.label: 3. Agentic AI: The Next Inflection Point
nodes.text: 3. Agentic AI: The Next Inflection Point
nodes.author: C4: Container, Code, Cloud & Context

nodes.label: 2025 saw agentic AI move from research labs to production. Unlike simple chatbot
nodes.text: 2025 saw agentic AI move from research labs to production. Unlike simple chatbots or single-purpose models, AI agents demonstrated the ability to:
nodes.author: C4: Container, Code, Cloud & Context

nodes.label: Plan complex, multi-step workflows autonomously without explicit instruction for
nodes.text: Plan complex, multi-step workflows autonomously without explicit instruction for each step
nodes.author: C4: Container, Code, Cloud & Context

nodes.label: Coordinate across multiple services and data sources, making intelligent decisio
nodes.text: Coordinate across multiple services and data sources, making intelligent decisions about which systems to query

nodes.text: Operate within defined guardrails while still demonstrating creativity and problem-solving

nodes.label: However, with this power came profound new challenges. The biggest question ente
nodes.text: However, with this power came profound new challenges. The biggest question enterprises grappled with: How do you audit an AI agent’s decision when it autonomously orchestrated 15 different microservices, made 23 API calls, and synthesized information from 7 data sources to complete a single task?

nodes.text: AI technology evolved so rapidly in 2025 that rigid architectures became obsolete within months. GPT-4 was state-of-the-art in January; by December, Claude 3.5, Gemini Pro, and dozens of specialized models had shifted the landscape entirely.

nodes.text: Test your architecture: Could you swap your primary LLM provider in under 8 hours? If not, you have architectural debt that will cost you dearly as the AI landscape continues to evolve.

nodes.label: The architectures that succeed will be those that treat AI as they would any oth
nodes.text: The architectures that succeed will be those that treat AI as they would any other enterprise capability: with rigorous governance, thoughtful integration, continuous monitoring, and unwavering focus on measurable business value.
nodes.author: C4: Container, Code, Cloud & Context

nodes.label: We’re past the point of asking “Should we use AI?” The question now is: “How do
nodes.text: We’re past the point of asking “Should we use AI?” The question now is: “How do we build AI systems that are trustworthy, cost-effective, compliant, and resilient enough to bet our business on?” That’s the challenge—and the opportunity—for 2026.
nodes.author: C4: Container, Code, Cloud & Context

nodes.label: For those of us who’ve spent decades building enterprise systems, it’s familiar
nodes.text: For those of us who’ve spent decades building enterprise systems, it’s familiar territory, just with new tools and higher stakes.
nodes.author: C4: Container, Code, Cloud & Context

nodes.label: | The Agentic Enterprise - The IT Architecture for the AI-Po
nodes.author: Salesforce Architects

nodes.label: This document presents a point of view describing the IT architecture that enter
nodes.text: This document presents a point of view describing the IT architecture that enterprises will need over the next 3-5 years to fully capture the value of an agentic workforce; it outlines the IT transformation required to support the large-scale deployment of AI agents.
nodes.author: Salesforce Architects

nodes.label: The goal is to provide a strategic guide and reference architecture to help CIOs
nodes.text: The goal is to provide a strategic guide and reference architecture to help CIOs, CDOs, and IT leaders plan their journey toward becoming an Agentic Enterprise.
nodes.author: Salesforce Architects

nodes.label: Powerful AI models are enabling the creation of an agentic workforce capable of
nodes.text: Powerful AI models are enabling the creation of an agentic workforce capable of sensing the environment, reasoning about data, making autonomous decisions, performing tasks, and effectively collaborating with human workers.
nodes.author: Salesforce Architects

nodes.label: This new workforce promises a step-change in innovation, productivity, and agili
nodes.text: This new workforce promises a step-change in innovation, productivity, and agility, creating value for shareholders and customers.
nodes.author: Salesforce Architects

nodes.label: To realize this vision, organizations must undergo a business and IT transformat
nodes.text: To realize this vision, organizations must undergo a business and IT transformation to become Agentic Enterprises.
nodes.author: Salesforce Architects

nodes.label: Today, the traditional enterprise faces operational inefficiencies arising from
nodes.text: Today, the traditional enterprise faces operational inefficiencies arising from information siloes, employees buried in manual work, misaligned incentives in organizational structures, and disjointed feedback loops between strategies and outcomes.
nodes.author: Salesforce Architects

nodes.label: These issues lead to suboptimal customer experiences, inefficient processes, and
nodes.text: These issues lead to suboptimal customer experiences, inefficient processes, and missed opportunities for growth.
nodes.author: Salesforce Architects

nodes.label: The Agentic Enterprise overcomes these limitations by integrating a digital work
nodes.text: The Agentic Enterprise overcomes these limitations by integrating a digital workforce of intelligent AI agents with human workers.
nodes.author: Salesforce Architects

nodes.label: With this new AI-augmented workforce, an organization can foster innovation for
nodes.text: With this new AI-augmented workforce, an organization can foster innovation for growth, drive operating excellence, and build enterprise resilience with several types of new business capabilities.
nodes.author: Salesforce Architects

nodes.label: New business capabilities to foster innovation:
nodes.text: New business capabilities to foster innovation:
nodes.author: Salesforce Architects

nodes.label: Augmented Human Productivity: The speed, scale, and always-on nature of AI agent
nodes.text: Augmented Human Productivity: The speed, scale, and always-on nature of AI agents allows enterprises to automate repetitive work, and free human workers to focus on higher-value and more creative tasks.
nodes.author: Salesforce Architects

nodes.label: Adaptive Capability Improvement: Because the reasoning of AI agents can be obser
nodes.text: Adaptive Capability Improvement: Because the reasoning of AI agents can be observed, they can dynamically learn and deploy new skills, enabling the enterprise to continuously improve performance to meet business goals and adapt quickly to new market opportunities.
nodes.author: Salesforce Architects

nodes.label: Example in Action — Innovating the Customer Experience in financial services: A
nodes.text: Example in Action — Innovating the Customer Experience in financial services: A wealth management firm can use an AI agent to reinvent its client engagement model.
nodes.author: Salesforce Architects

nodes.label: The agent autonomously monitors portfolios, identifies key moments for a client
nodes.text: The agent autonomously monitors portfolios, identifies key moments for a client review, and prepares the pre-call plan for the advisor, adjusting the plan as news emerges.
nodes.author: Salesforce Architects

nodes.label: This allows the human advisor to deliver a proactive, personalized customer expe
nodes.text: This allows the human advisor to deliver a proactive, personalized customer experience at scale, strengthening relationships and uncovering new opportunities.
nodes.author: Salesforce Architects

nodes.label: New business capabilities to protect and ensure organizational resiliency:
nodes.text: New business capabilities to protect and ensure organizational resiliency:
nodes.author: Salesforce Architects

nodes.label: Elastic Workforce Capacity: Enterprises can quickly scale their ability to meet
nodes.text: Elastic Workforce Capacity: Enterprises can quickly scale their ability to meet surges in workload from changing business conditions, without the costs and delays of ramping up a entirely human workforce.
nodes.author: Salesforce Architects

nodes.label: Predictive Operational Resilience: The 24/7 nature of AI agents enables them to
nodes.text: Predictive Operational Resilience: The 24/7 nature of AI agents enables them to autonomously anticipate, model, and mitigate operational, compliance, and security risks in real-time, ensuring the enterprise maintains the trust of its customers and stakeholders.
nodes.author: Salesforce Architects

nodes.label: Example in Action — Protecting customer data: A large enterprise can deploy an A
nodes.text: Example in Action — Protecting customer data: A large enterprise can deploy an AI data governance agent to scan the regulatory environment for changes in data privacy laws, discover and classify sensitive information in enterprise data sets, and then apply the appropriate governance policy.
nodes.author: Salesforce Architects

nodes.label: The agent can review data access requests and route exceptions to a human analys
nodes.text: The agent can review data access requests and route exceptions to a human analyst for review, reducing compliance risk while enabling data to be used in a trusted manner.
nodes.author: Salesforce Architects

nodes.label: New business capabilities that optimize operating excellence:
nodes.text: New business capabilities that optimize operating excellence:
nodes.author: Salesforce Architects

nodes.label: Autonomous Process Execution: A digital workforce can execute complex, multi-ste
nodes.text: Autonomous Process Execution: A digital workforce can execute complex, multi-step tasks 24/7 at machine speed (with humans in the loop), reducing costs and scaling processes efficiently.
nodes.author: Salesforce Architects

nodes.label: Transboundary Orchestration: AI agents can work across the information and incen
nodes.text: Transboundary Orchestration: AI agents can work across the information and incentive silos that normally constrain human workers, fostering agility for cross-functional processes.
nodes.author: Salesforce Architects

nodes.label: Example in Action — Optimizing the marketing funnel in retail: A retail marketin
nodes.text: Example in Action — Optimizing the marketing funnel in retail: A retail marketing team can deploy an AI agent to accelerate its campaign process in response to new consumer trends.
nodes.author: Salesforce Architects

nodes.label: The agent can generate marketing plans, collaborate with marketing, product, and
nodes.text: The agent can generate marketing plans, collaborate with marketing, product, and sales teams for review, and then automatically create digital collateral and execute across multiple channels, dynamically adjusting the campaign based on real-time feedback.
nodes.author: Salesforce Architects

nodes.label: The IT architecture of the enterprise can be depicted using a layer construct.
nodes.text: The IT architecture of the enterprise can be depicted using a layer construct.
nodes.author: Salesforce Architects

nodes.label: The layers logically group related technical functionality and facilitate struct
nodes.text: The layers logically group related technical functionality and facilitate structured reasoning, but do not necessarily imply specific implementations or the degree to which a layer should be designed monolithically or in a more heterogeneous manner.
nodes.author: Salesforce Architects

nodes.label: In this layer view (Figure 1), the traditional IT architecture consists of five
nodes.text: In this layer view (Figure 1), the traditional IT architecture consists of five main layers: Infrastructure, Data, Integration, Application, and Experience.
nodes.author: Salesforce Architects

nodes.label: Two cross-layers, Security and IT Operations, span these layers to ensure govern
nodes.text: Two cross-layers, Security and IT Operations, span these layers to ensure governance, monitoring, and protection.
nodes.author: Salesforce Architects

nodes.label: The traditional IT architecture was designed for a paradigm where the enterprise
nodes.text: The traditional IT architecture was designed for a paradigm where the enterprise’s intelligence resided with human workers who took actions in applications to access data, apply business logic, facilitate collaboration, and execute workflows.
nodes.author: Salesforce Architects

nodes.label: It is not designed for a paradigm where AI agents can reason and take actions fo
nodes.text: It is not designed for a paradigm where AI agents can reason and take actions for certain use cases previously done by humans (or not done at all) while humans supervise the AI agents and focus on more creative and ambiguous tasks.
nodes.author: Salesforce Architects

nodes.label: While traditional architecture may support sub-scale deployments of AI agents to
nodes.text: While traditional architecture may support sub-scale deployments of AI agents today, it cannot fully deliver the business capabilities of the Agentic Enterprise described above.
nodes.author: Salesforce Architects

nodes.label: Realizing these capabilities requires an IT architecture designed for wide-sprea
nodes.text: Realizing these capabilities requires an IT architecture designed for wide-spread deployment of powerful AI agents that can address broad use cases instead of being restricted to limited deployments targeting narrow use cases.
nodes.author: Salesforce Architects

nodes.label: AI agents will continue to improve over the next 5 years, and the IT architectur
nodes.text: AI agents will continue to improve over the next 5 years, and the IT architecture will need to evolve in order to realize the value of more powerful and intelligent AI agents, to become future proof.
nodes.author: Salesforce Architects

nodes.label: First, agents will become more intelligent as the underlying AI models (such as
nodes.text: First, agents will become more intelligent as the underlying AI models (such as multi-modal LLMs) and the agents’ cognitive architectures evolve (for example, multi-step planning, task decomposition, and so on).
nodes.author: Salesforce Architects

nodes.label: Second, AI agents will have improved learning and adaptation abilities with memo
nodes.text: Second, AI agents will have improved learning and adaptation abilities with memory improvements, self-reflection capabilities, and the ability to learn from feedback.
nodes.author: Salesforce Architects

nodes.label: Third, AI agents will have a greater ability to interact with other agents, tool
nodes.text: Third, AI agents will have a greater ability to interact with other agents, tools, and data as evidenced by the fast evolving ecosystem of open technology standards (for example Model Context Protocol, Agent2Agent, and so on).
nodes.author: Salesforce Architects

nodes.label: While these three technology trends will enable AI agents to be more powerful as
nodes.text: While these three technology trends will enable AI agents to be more powerful as they execute more abstract and complex tasks, it will also introduce numerous challenges for today’s IT architecture.
nodes.author: Salesforce Architects

nodes.label: First, AI agents fundamentally rely on AI models, both internally developed and
nodes.text: First, AI agents fundamentally rely on AI models, both internally developed and externally sourced, which evolve rapidly and demand sophisticated, shared, and standardized AI/ML model management.
nodes.author: Salesforce Architects

nodes.label: Today, AI models are bolted on for specific use cases in an application, not as
nodes.text: Today, AI models are bolted on for specific use cases in an application, not as shared capabilities for reuse with common tooling for training, deployment, governance and risk management.
nodes.author: Salesforce Architects

nodes.label: Going forward, enterprises will need to be able to use different AI models for v
nodes.text: Going forward, enterprises will need to be able to use different AI models for various agentic use cases which require tooling that enables agents to swap out underlying models (e.g., foundation model vs. domain-specific smaller model) based on business context.
nodes.author: Salesforce Architects

nodes.label: This necessitates managing internally developed or hosted AI models with unified
nodes.text: This necessitates managing internally developed or hosted AI models with unified lifecycle tooling to ensure consistency, reusability, scalability, and efficiency.
nodes.author: Salesforce Architects

nodes.label: Similarly, accessing externally hosted AI models necessitates an enterprise-wide
nodes.text: Similarly, accessing externally hosted AI models necessitates an enterprise-wide control framework to ensure optimal performance, security, compliance, availability, and reliability.
nodes.author: Salesforce Architects

nodes.label: Second, AI agents have distinct scaling patterns and operational requirements su
nodes.text: Second, AI agents have distinct scaling patterns and operational requirements such as hosting, development, reasoning, learning, memory management, and operations, which necessitate a separate and dedicated architectural boundary for agents.
nodes.author: Salesforce Architects

nodes.label: Embedding this functionality directly in today’s static and deterministic applic
nodes.text: Embedding this functionality directly in today’s static and deterministic application architecture would introduce unnecessary architectural complexity and risk.
nodes.author: Salesforce Architects

nodes.label: Moreover, AI agents should interoperate with existing applications through stand
nodes.text: Moreover, AI agents should interoperate with existing applications through standardized interfaces or messaging systems for real-time interaction.
nodes.author: Salesforce Architects

nodes.label: Third, AI agents need to be able to reason over disparate data sets and collabor
nodes.text: Third, AI agents need to be able to reason over disparate data sets and collaborate with each other, often across siloed application stacks, but in today’s architecture, there’s no common semantic functionality to provide a shared understanding for these agents to reason over different data sets.
nodes.author: Salesforce Architects

nodes.label: As a result, although single-purpose agents can be deployed successfully, scalin
nodes.text: As a result, although single-purpose agents can be deployed successfully, scaling them to operate in large numbers on complex, cross-silo tasks remains difficult and risky.
nodes.author: Salesforce Architects

nodes.label: Lastly, the current enterprise IT architecture lacks an effective way to orchest
nodes.text: Lastly, the current enterprise IT architecture lacks an effective way to orchestrate, optimize, and govern end-to-end business processes that include the dynamic workflows performed by more powerful agents, which will augment and in some cases replace the role done by human workers in that process.
nodes.author: Salesforce Architects

nodes.label: Today, automation tools are leveraged to manage linear, deterministic workflows
nodes.text: Today, automation tools are leveraged to manage linear, deterministic workflows that typically follow a predefined sequence, documented in process-specific languages, and rely on static logic that rarely changes.
nodes.author: Salesforce Architects

nodes.label: AI technologies can enhance some of these linear processes (for example, using M
nodes.text: AI technologies can enhance some of these linear processes (for example, using ML models instead of hardcoded business rules to calculate loan approval thresholds), but the strategic and execution aspects of most critical business processes remain inherently dynamic and flexible.
nodes.author: Salesforce Architects

nodes.label: Tasks such as developing marketing strategies, resolving complex customer issues
nodes.text: Tasks such as developing marketing strategies, resolving complex customer issues, or prospecting clients have clear objectives (customer satisfaction, case resolution speed, and so on) but do not follow a fixed, predefined sequence of execution.
nodes.author: Salesforce Architects

nodes.label: Currently, traditional enterprises primarily rely on humans to coordinate and pe
nodes.text: Currently, traditional enterprises primarily rely on humans to coordinate and perform these complex business processes (such as setting strategy and managing complex programs).
nodes.author: Salesforce Architects

nodes.label: As AI agents continue to evolve (greater intelligence, learning, and interaction
nodes.text: As AI agents continue to evolve (greater intelligence, learning, and interaction capabilities) over the next 3-5 years, their ability to execute such dynamic processes autonomously will significantly expand, introducing complexities and integration challenges far exceeding the capabilities of existing integration and automation tools.
nodes.author: Salesforce Architects

nodes.label: The adaptive and dynamic nature of AI agents creates a strong need for novel orc
nodes.text: The adaptive and dynamic nature of AI agents creates a strong need for novel orchestration capabilities to ensure enterprise-level control, comprehensive visibility, and consistent alignment with enterprise-wide strategic objectives, particularly in managing complex, lengthy, and multi-step workflows that encompass AI agents, humans, automation tools, and other deterministic systems.
nodes.author: Salesforce Architects

nodes.label: The IT architecture of the Agentic Enterprise establishes a platform for intelli
nodes.text: The IT architecture of the Agentic Enterprise establishes a platform for intelligent actions by seamlessly integrating human workers, AI agents, and deterministic systems.
nodes.author: Salesforce Architects

nodes.label: This architecture empowers both human and AI agents to dynamically access and le
nodes.text: This architecture empowers both human and AI agents to dynamically access and leverage unified enterprise knowledge from diverse data sources, enriched with semantic context to efficiently execute complex workflows and processes aligned with strategic business goals.
nodes.author: Salesforce Architects

nodes.label: The existing IT architecture of a set of siloed platforms and point solutions wi
nodes.text: The existing IT architecture of a set of siloed platforms and point solutions will evolve towards a set of composable application services, semantic and data tools, and networks of AI agents overseen and governed by new intelligent business process orchestration tools.
nodes.author: Salesforce Architects

nodes.label: This architecture enables agents to sense, reason, and act within their respecti
nodes.text: This architecture enables agents to sense, reason, and act within their respective scopes, work within and across business domains, and continuously learn, improve, and adapt.
nodes.author: Salesforce Architects

nodes.label: This necessitates a design focused on robust mechanisms to access data and knowl
nodes.text: This necessitates a design focused on robust mechanisms to access data and knowledge (semantic understanding), flexible and standardized communication protocols and interfaces (such as agent to agent, agent to deterministic systems, and agent to human) and critically, orchestrate workflows and processes across agents, humans, and automation tools and deterministic systems.
nodes.author: Salesforce Architects

nodes.label: To realize the architectural vision of a platform for intelligent actions, these
nodes.text: To realize the architectural vision of a platform for intelligent actions, these design principles are recommended as best practice:
nodes.author: Salesforce Architects

nodes.label: Composability and modularity: Design architectural elements as modular component
nodes.text: Composability and modularity: Design architectural elements as modular components with standardized interfaces to enable rapid and dynamic assembly of agent capabilities, workflows, and human facing surfaces. Prioritize clear interface contracts and abstraction to enable the greatest flexibility for AI agents to compose workflows.
nodes.author: Salesforce Architects

nodes.label: Data and semantic first: Ensure comprehensive, accurate, fast, secure, and cost-
nodes.text: Data and semantic first: Ensure comprehensive, accurate, fast, secure, and cost-effective access to data, with shared semantic understanding for agents to effectively reason across siloed systems. This requires treating data (and metadata) as a product, with tools to ensure quality, lineage, governance, and access as well as a way to provide semantic understanding shared across agents and humans.
nodes.author: Salesforce Architects

nodes.label: IT and business observability embedded: Embed end-to-end monitoring, tracing, ev
nodes.text: IT and business observability embedded: Embed end-to-end monitoring, tracing, evaluation, and explainability capabilities within the entire architecture for insight into agents’ reasoning, behaviors, system interactions, and impact on business KPIs to enable the continuous optimization of agents’ performance. This includes cost optimization (FinOps), sustainability metrics, and operational telemetry while maintaining trust, compliance, and responsible resource consumption. Since AI agents are inherently non-deterministic, observability is paramount to ensure AI agents can operate in a trusted, compliant, and auditable manner with human oversight.
nodes.author: Salesforce Architects

nodes.label: Trust-throughout: Enforce dynamic, granular permissions based on the intent of t
nodes.text: Trust-throughout: Enforce dynamic, granular permissions based on the intent of the agent’s tasks (data access, actions, and so on) and implement comprehensive security practices including red teaming, automated CVE scanning, vulnerability detection, and risk-based validation controls. More granular and dynamic controls are needed given the risk of agents causing cascading risks due to their ability to operate at machine speeds. Ensure all AI-generated outputs (from agents or models) are rigorously validated against defined policies for compliance, safety, toxicity, and bias before use or delivery, requiring logging and explainability mechanisms with verifiable audit trails for AI decisions, actions, content, and predictions.
nodes.author: Salesforce Architects

nodes.label: Agent-first with human oversight: Enable AI agents to be the default tool to sol
nodes.text: Agent-first with human oversight: Enable AI agents to be the default tool to solve business use cases, barring other considerations (e.g., cost, technical fit), and design IT systems to be accessible for agentic workflows. This includes the ability for humans to monitor, intervene in, and override any step of an agent's process. Agents require self-reflective capabilities to proactively seek human guidance if its confidence over decision-making falls below pre-programmed thresholds.
nodes.author: Salesforce Architects

nodes.label: Reactive and multimodal interaction: Enable the architecture to support comprehe
nodes.text: Reactive and multimodal interaction: Enable the architecture to support comprehensive agent invocation and response mechanisms across all interaction types including agent-to-agent protocols, human multimodal inputs (voice, text, visual), business events, system signals, and streaming data. Enable both event-driven and real-time processing capabilities to ensure agents can react to any timely signal from any source or format.
nodes.author: Salesforce Architects

nodes.label: AI-ready infrastructure: Ensure infrastructure can elastically scale with redund
nodes.text: AI-ready infrastructure: Ensure infrastructure can elastically scale with redundancy built in to handle fluctuating AI workloads, and integrate ML/LLM pipelines into data and application architecture, while maintaining compliance with data residency requirements.
nodes.author: Salesforce Architects

nodes.label: Open ecosystem: Prioritize interoperability and avoid technology lock-in by favo
nodes.text: Open ecosystem: Prioritize interoperability and avoid technology lock-in by favoring open standards, protocols, and well-defined interfaces (APIs, events) to benefit from the technology ecosystem.
nodes.author: Salesforce Architects

nodes.label: To successfully enable and scale the agentic transformation, enterprises must go
nodes.text: To successfully enable and scale the agentic transformation, enterprises must go beyond merely enhancing current layers; they need to consider explicitly introducing four additional architectural layers (Figure 2) designed specifically to meet the needs of AI agents.
nodes.author: Salesforce Architects

nodes.label: The Agentic Layer is dedicated to the development and management of AI agents, e
nodes.text: The Agentic Layer is dedicated to the development and management of AI agents, encompassing cognitive capabilities such as planning, reasoning, memory, tool utilization, state management, and lifecycle control.
nodes.author: Salesforce Architects

nodes.label: This layer addresses the unique technical and operational requirements of AI age
nodes.text: This layer addresses the unique technical and operational requirements of AI agents, ensures interoperability across applications and data stores through standardized protocols, and facilitates agent-to-agent collaboration.
nodes.author: Salesforce Architects

nodes.label: The existing application layer will evolve into application services to be dynam
nodes.text: The existing application layer will evolve into application services to be dynamically composed for agentic workflows.
nodes.author: Salesforce Architects

nodes.label: The Semantic Layer is introduced to resolve the disconnect between raw enterpris
nodes.text: The Semantic Layer is introduced to resolve the disconnect between raw enterprise data and the semantic understanding that AI agents need.
nodes.author: Salesforce Architects

nodes.label: It explicitly encodes and manages business entities, concepts, definitions, and
nodes.text: It explicitly encodes and manages business entities, concepts, definitions, and inter-relationships, creating an enterprise ontology and representation of business knowledge to enable shared semantic understanding that power more complex multi-agent workflows performing higher-level tasks.
nodes.author: Salesforce Architects

nodes.label: Beyond a data catalog, the Semantic Layer translates a natural language query in
nodes.text: Beyond a data catalog, the Semantic Layer translates a natural language query into precise queries against specific data stores, harmonizes the results, and returns a contextual and richer answer for the agent.
nodes.author: Salesforce Architects

nodes.label: The existing Data Layer meanwhile unifies via adoption of centralized lakehouses
nodes.text: The existing Data Layer meanwhile unifies via adoption of centralized lakehouses while broadening data access via an AI-ready data fabric to support data mesh operating model principles.
nodes.author: Salesforce Architects

nodes.label: The AI/ML Layer centralizes the management of enterprise AI capabilities, includ
nodes.text: The AI/ML Layer centralizes the management of enterprise AI capabilities, including large language models, large action models and domain-specific ML models, handling both internally developed AI models throughout their lifecycle and the controlled access/usage to external AI services.
nodes.author: Salesforce Architects

nodes.label: Unlike traditional architectures where AI models are embedded within application
nodes.text: Unlike traditional architectures where AI models are embedded within applications, this layer establishes AI models as first-class components and shared services in the enterprise.
nodes.author: Salesforce Architects

nodes.label: It focuses on enterprise-controlled AI capabilities (not AI capabilities provide
nodes.text: It focuses on enterprise-controlled AI capabilities (not AI capabilities provided by external vendors).
nodes.author: Salesforce Architects

nodes.label: This layer provides the intelligence for various agents and other AI workloads i
nodes.text: This layer provides the intelligence for various agents and other AI workloads in the enterprise with standardized mechanisms for trust, safety, compliance, and deployment.
nodes.author: Salesforce Architects

nodes.label: The Enterprise Orchestration Layer is the functional abstraction for coordinatin
